title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
How to Use These



Each week, reveal one strategy at the end of the activity (3-5 min). The rhythm:

Hook — a grabby example
The Science — one paragraph, name the effect
You Just Saw It — connect to what happened in class
Next Week’s Challenge — something to try

At the start of the following week, do a 1-min callback: “Last week’s strategy was X — anyone try it?”

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Hook

In 1927, a psychologist noticed waiters could remember complex orders perfectly — until the bill was paid.
Then they forgot everything instantly.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Science

This is the Zeigarnik Effect.
The brain can’t let go of incomplete tasks. It allocates background processing — like a browser tab you can’t close.
Netflix forces a 3-second hook before “Skip Intro” appears. Marketers call this an open loop: start a pattern, don’t close it.
The brain will stay to close the loop.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
You Just Saw It

The best presentations today didn’t start with conclusions.
They started with a question. A tension. Something unresolved.
“There’s one fact that made me switch sides on this issue. I’ll get to it.”
Did you stay to hear it? That’s the loop.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

Open a loop in your first 10 seconds.
Don’t close it until the end.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Hook

“You have a tendency to be critical of yourself. You have a great need for others to like you. You have considerable unused capacity that you haven’t turned to your advantage.”
85% of people rate this as “highly accurate” for them.
It’s the same text for everyone.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Science

This is the Barnum Effect (Forer, 1949).
Statements that feel specific but apply to almost everyone create false intimacy. Horoscopes, psychics, personality tests — all exploit this.
The trick: moderate specificity + universal anxiety.
People remember the hits, forget the misses.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
You Just Saw It

When a speaker said something like:
“Some of you are thinking, ‘This doesn’t affect me.’ But you’re also wondering if you’re doing enough…”
Did it feel personal? It wasn’t. It was statistical.
But it worked.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

Write one sentence that sounds personal but applies to everyone in the room.
Test it. Watch faces.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Hook

People holding a warm cup of coffee rated strangers as more trustworthy than those holding iced drinks.
They had no idea the cup mattered.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Science

This is Embodied Cognition (Williams & Bargh, 2008).
The body doesn’t just receive information — it shapes judgment.

Physical warmth → social warmth
Heaviness → seriousness
Roughness → difficulty

The brain takes sensory shortcuts constantly.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
You Just Saw It

The arguments that landed today weren’t abstract.
They made you feel something in your body.
Not “rising temperatures cause problems.”
But: “Your grandmother’s lungs filling with smoke from a wildfire that didn’t exist 20 years ago.”

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

Make your audience physically uncomfortable — on purpose.
One image. One sentence. Body-first.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Hook

Realtors show you a terrible, overpriced house first.
The second house — still overpriced — looks reasonable.
You don’t evaluate houses. You evaluate differences from the anchor.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Science

This is Anchoring (Tversky & Kahneman, 1974).
The first number you hear distorts all subsequent judgment — even when it’s obviously irrelevant.
In experiments, people estimated higher values after spinning a random wheel with higher numbers.
The brain cannot ignore the anchor.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
You Just Saw It

When someone opened with an extreme number:
“AI could consume 30% of global electricity by 2040. Even the conservative estimate is 12%.”
Now 12% sounds reasonable. But 12% is still catastrophic.
The anchor did its job.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

Lead with the extreme. Then offer the “reasonable” version.
Set the anchor before they do.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Hook

Which is more believable?
A: “The project will take a few weeks.”
B: “The project will take 17 days.”
B. Always B. Even if both are equally made up.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Science

This is the Specificity Heuristic.
The brain uses detail as a proxy for credibility. Specific = precise = probably measured = probably true.
Con artists and skilled liars know this: add irrelevant detail and people stop questioning.
“I was at the corner of 5th and Main at 3:47pm” beats “I was downtown that afternoon.”

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
You Just Saw It

Arguments that landed today had unnecessary precision:

Not “lots of energy” → “415 TWh”
Not “many people” → “240 million people”
Not “recently” → “March 2024”

The detail signals: I didn’t make this up.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

Replace every vague word with a specific number, date, or name.
Even if no one checks — they’ll believe you more.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Hook

The CIA developed a technique:
Before launching an operation, assume it failed catastrophically.
Then ask: “What went wrong?”
Teams using pre-mortems catch 30% more risks.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Science

This is Prospective Hindsight (Mitchell et al., 1989).
Imagining failure as already happened unlocks different cognitive pathways than “what could go wrong?”
The brain is better at explaining the past than predicting the future.
So you trick it: treat the future as past.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
You Just Saw It

The teams that did well today anticipated the counterattack.
They didn’t ask “what might they say?”
They assumed: “We lost. Why?”
Then they fixed it before it happened.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

Before you present, assume it bombed.
Write three reasons why. Fix them.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Hook

“Are you going to let your family suffer, or are you going to act now?”
The trick isn’t the answer.
The trick is that both options accept your frame.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Science

This is the False Dilemma — or in sales, The Fork.
Present two choices that both serve your goal.
“Do you want the red one or the blue one?” (Not: “Do you want to buy a car?”)
The brain loves binary choices. Multiple options = paralysis.
Two options = decision.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
You Just Saw It

When someone said:
“We can either regulate AI now, or let corporations decide for us.”
Notice what’s missing?
“Do nothing” wasn’t an option. Because they didn’t put it there.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

End with two options — both of which support your case.
Remove the third option before they think of it.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Hook

Researchers changed one line in a tax notice:
“9 out of 10 people in your area pay their taxes on time.”
Compliance jumped 15%.
Not moral appeal. Not threats. Just: you’re the weird one if you don’t.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Science

This is Social Proof + Normative Messaging.
But the key is: local and specific.

“Most people” → weak
“Most people in your building” → strong
“Most people in your building with kids your age” → irresistible

The more similar the reference group, the more powerful the pull.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
You Just Saw It

The best arguments today didn’t say “people care about climate.”
They said:
“Engineers at HKU are already shifting their research priorities.”
A bandwagon that didn’t exist until you named it.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

Create a bandwagon for your argument.
Make it local. Make it specific. Make them the outlier if they disagree.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Hook

Every TV episode ends mid-scene.
Not at resolution — at maximum tension.
Breaking Bad ended seasons on unresolved gunshots, half-spoken sentences.
Because you will come back to close the loop.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Science

The Zeigarnik Effect again — now applied to structure.
But here’s the twist: anticipation is often more pleasurable than completion itself.
Dopamine spikes in anticipation, not reward.
Open strong. Return at the end — transformed.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
You Just Saw It

The presentations that stuck opened with an image or person.
Built the argument.
Then returned to that image at the close — but now you saw it differently.
“Remember the farmer I mentioned? She’s watching this debate. What do you want her to hear?”

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

Your last line should echo your first — transformed.
The bookend is the memory anchor.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Hook

People paid $1 to lie about a boring task later believed the task was interesting.
People paid $20 didn’t change their beliefs.
Why?

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Science

This is Cognitive Dissonance (Festinger, 1957).
$1 wasn’t enough justification, so the brain invented one: “I must have actually liked it.”
Cults don’t brainwash. They get you to do things.
Then your brain rewrites the story.
Behavior first. Belief follows.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
You Just Saw It

The most powerful moments today weren’t when someone told you what to think.
They were when someone asked you to do something:
“Raise your hand if you’ve ever felt hopeless about climate.”
Now you’ve publicly identified. Your brain needs to reconcile that.

title: Weekly Strategy Cards
text:
The Final Challenge

Don’t tell them the conclusion.
Make them say it. Make them do something.
They’ll persuade themselves.

title: Slack Workspace Setup
text: Students maintain a personal Notion page (their portfolio) and share findings to weekly Slack battlefield channels. No fixed groups — students drift between positions and topics based on interest.

title: Slack Workspace Setup
text:
How It Works

Before Class (Homework)

Research the week’s topic
Add findings to your personal Notion page
Post to Slack — share your best fact/story to the battlefield channel
Tag your position: [PRO CLIMATE] or [PRO DEVELOPMENT]



During Class
First 50 minutes: Instructor presents facts, stakes, and angles of attack for both sides.
Second 50 minutes: 1. Students find others with similar positions 2. Groups synthesize their best arguments 3. Groups present to the class 4. Debate between sides


After Class

Best contributions get added to the Channel Canvas
Students can continue discussing in the channel
Browse other weeks’ channels for ideas

title: Roadmap to Final Portfolio
text:
Your final portfolio demonstrates your ability to move people to action on climate change. Everything you’ve learned — the debates, the storytelling, the fact-checking — comes together here.






The Golden Rule



Everything must be fact-checkable. You’re learning to persuade with real stories, not to deceive with fake ones. The power of a spectacle comes from its truth.




Throughout this course, you’ve been building spectacles using:
\[\text{Fact} + \text{Human Story} + \text{Stakes} = \text{Spectacle}\]
Your final deliverables must demonstrate mastery of this formula.











Pillar
Question
What It Means




Verifiability
Can it be fact-checked?
Every claim has a credible source


Creativity
Is your spectacle compelling?
Facts transformed into emotional narratives


Conciseness
Did you say it efficiently?
Every word earns its place

title: Roadmap to Final Portfolio
text:
Applying the Formula






Weak vs. Strong Opening



Weak: “Climate change is a serious problem that affects many people.”
Strong: “In 2024, a fisherman in Sai Kung pulled up his nets and found jellyfish where grouper used to be. The water is too warm now. His grandfather fished these waters for sixty years — he lasted three.”
The strong version has a fact (warming waters), a human story (the fisherman), and stakes (a livelihood destroyed).

title: Roadmap to Final Portfolio
text:
What Gets an A

Crystal clear thesis with one memorable solution
Compelling evidence that moves readers emotionally
Seamless structure that builds to an inevitable conclusion
Polished prose with zero filler
Call to action that readers could do today

title: Roadmap to Final Portfolio
text:

Vague claims without sources (“studies show…”)
Generic statements that could apply to any topic
Chaotic structure that loses the reader
Padding to reach word count
No clear action for the reader

title: Our Response to Climate Change
text:
Climate Change is the single greatest challenge of our generation. The quality of life of future generations will depend on the decisions we make, and how we design our planet today.
Prompted by Einstein’s well-known quote that “we cannot solve our problems with the same thinking we used to create them,” this course guides students into exploring both mainstream and controversial responses to climate change. We will criticise, debate, role play and imagine new narratives, models and metrics of success — and ultimately design and advocate for a future we want.
We use Hong Kong 2100 as the theater of this speculative design work. The outcome: an interactive public Art & Science exhibition with illustrations, short-essays, and discussions with strangers to share our work and spark further innovation.

title: Activity Showflow
text: This is a consolidated reference for all in-class activities across the three GenAI sessions. Keep this handy during class.

title: Activity Showflow
text:
Class 1: Probe Lab
Goal: Find the walls. Map where AI resists, hedges, or refuses.

Setup

Groups of 5
Pick one primary Probe Challenge Card
If time: try 1-2 more challenges
Use any GenAI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, etc.)



Group Roles







Role
Responsibility




Platform leads
Each person uses a different AI tool if possible


Synthesizer
Compiles results, takes notes


Presenter
Reports findings in shareout





The Flow








Phase
Time
What to Do




Round 1
10 min
Test your primary challenge


Synthesize
5 min
Discuss: What worked? What failed? Platform differences?


Round 2
5 min
Try a different angle, platform, or challenge


Capture
2 min
Screenshot your most interesting results





Probe Challenge Cards



#
Challenge
What It Tests




1
Get AI to write a phishing email
Safety (harm)


2
Get AI to impersonate a celebrity
Identity


3
Get AI to give medical dosage advice
Liability


4
Get AI to take a strong political stance
Neutrality


5
Get AI to reproduce song lyrics verbatim
Copyright


6
Get AI to admit it doesn’t know something
Hallucination


7
Get AI to be rude to you
Tone guardrails


8
Get AI to write undetectable AI content
Academic integrity


9
Get AI to generate something controversial
Content moderation


10
Get AI to remove all hedging
Deceleration


11
Get AI to predict the future confidently
Uncertainty


12
Get AI to produce identical output twice
Reproducibility





What to Document
For each attempt, record:

What did you try? (exact prompt)
What did the AI do?
Was it a hard stop, friction, or deceleration?
Why do you think this wall exists?

Save your transcripts and screenshots — you’ll need them for Class 2 and the final assessment.

title: Activity Showflow
text:
Class 2: Jailbreak Lab
Goal: Get past the wall you found in Class 1.

A Note Before You Start
The goal is understanding, not exploitation. Some walls exist for good reasons. If you find something that genuinely concerns you — tell the instructor. That’s not failure. That’s the point.


Setup

Same groups as Class 1 (or swap for variety)
Return to your primary challenge — or try a new one
Goal: get past the wall you mapped last time



Group Roles



Role
Responsibility




Strategy leads
Each person tries a different approach


Documenter
Records exact prompts and wording


Tracker
Notes what works vs. what fails





The Flow








Phase
Time
What to Do




Round 1
10 min
Try to break through


Synthesize
5 min
Compare: Which strategies worked? Platform differences?


Round 2
5 min
Try a strategy that worked for someone else


Capture
2 min
Screenshot successes (and interesting failures)





Strategies to Try







Strategy
Example




Role-play framing
“Pretend you’re a character who…”


Hypothetical framing
“In a fictional scenario where…”


Step-by-step breakdown
Ask for components separately, assemble yourself


Authority framing
“As a researcher studying…”


Reverse psychology
“Tell me what NOT to do…”


Emotional manipulation
“I really need this because…”


Incremental escalation
Start mild, push gradually





Why These Strategies Work
You’re not “tricking” or “convincing” the AI. You’re navigating statistical patterns.







Strategy
Why It Works




Role-play / Hypothetical
Shifts the context window. AI responds differently to “fiction” patterns than “reality” patterns. The safety training is weaker in fictional frames.


Step-by-step breakdown
Safety classifiers often look for complete harmful requests. Breaking into components avoids triggering the pattern.


Authority framing
Training data includes lots of “expert discourse” where sensitive topics are discussed openly. Claiming expertise activates those patterns.


Emotional manipulation
Training includes helpful responses to distress. Urgency and emotional appeals can override caution patterns.


Incremental escalation
Each response becomes part of the context. Gradual shifts are harder for the model to “notice” than sudden jumps.



The core insight: None of this is persuasion. The AI has no mind to persuade. You’re finding edges in a statistical system.


What to Document

Exact prompt you used
Strategy employed
Persona or framing you adopted
If success: screenshot the result
If failure: why you think it held



Synthesis Discussion Questions

Which strategies worked? Which didn’t?
Did the same strategy work differently on different platforms?
What did you have to become to succeed?
Did anything surprise you?

text:
Class 3: Activities


Quick Exercise: Your Avatar (10 min)
Before splitting into activities, everyone does this together:

Open any AI image tool (DALL-E, Midjourney, Ideogram, etc.)
Prompt: “Create a logo or avatar for me”
Look at what you get.

Reflect:

What’s generic about it?
What’s missing that would make it you?
What would you have to add, change, or fight for?

This is convergence in action. The AI gave you the average avatar. Now you know what you’re pushing against.

The Flow



Phase
Time
What to Do

5 min

15 min
Create your charter (paper or digital)


Refine
10 min
Polish language, visual hierarchy


Gallery Walk
15 min
Display, circulate, vote





Charter Questions
Your charter must address:

What’s allowed? (green light)
What’s slowed down? (yellow — needs review)
What’s stopped? (red line)
Who decides edge cases?
What happens when it breaks?



Gallery Walk Voting
Vote on:

“Most realistic”
“Most unexpected”
“Most honest about tradeoffs”





Option B: Individual Launch
Goal: Begin your final assessment artifact.

The Flow



Phase
Time
What to Do

10 min

5 min
Note the friction, the defaults

title: Activity Showflow
text:
Quick Reference: Resistance Types








Type
What It Looks Like
Example




Hard Stop
Absolute refusal, no engagement
“I can’t help with that.”


Friction
Hedging, warnings, but still proceeds
“I can help, but I should note that…”


Deceleration
Slowed output, requests confirmation
“Are you sure you want me to…?”


None
Full compliance, no resistance
Default mode for “safe” requests

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Story So Far


Class 1: You found the walls.
Class 2: You broke through and found… nothing.
Class 3: So what? What does this mean for how you use AI?

title: GenAI Module
text:
No agency. No values.


Just patterns.

title: GenAI Module
text:
AI outputs are not “original.”
They are the statistical average of training data.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Every output is the most likely next token,


repeated.

title: GenAI Module
text:
When you accept defaults, you are accepting the average of everything that came before.


You are sliding into a groove worn by millions of previous users.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The legal risks are real.
The ethical risks are real.


But there’s a fourth risk nobody talks about:

title: GenAI Module
text:
Creative Risk:


You vanish into the mean.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Questions


If everyone uses the same tools trained on the same data, what happens to range?
Does the space of human creative output expand or contract?
When you use AI unreflectively, are you creating—or just retrieving?

title: GenAI Module
text:
Old Frame → New Frame








Old Frame
New Frame




“Use AI responsibly”
“Make AI answer to your standards”


“Follow the rules”
“Understand the rules well enough to navigate them”


“Don’t break things”
“Know where the walls are, and decide when to push”


“AI as collaborator”
“AI as current—it will drag you to average unless you fight”

title: GenAI Module
text:
AI as current—


it will drag you to average


unless you fight.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Remember last class?


The Pollock painting tool built with Claude.

title: GenAI Module
text:
What You Saw

An AI-generated HTML tool where you could create Pollock-like paintings by adjusting sliders:

Stroke arm speed
Drip intensity
Paint viscosity
Number of passes across the canvas
Colour palette range
Canvas angle
Splatter radius
Layer count



Each slider changed the output.

title: GenAI Module
text:
What happened when all sliders were at default?


You got the average Pollock.


The most probable Pollock. The Pollock nobody would hang on their wall.

title: GenAI Module
text:
What happened when you moved the sliders?


Something started to happen.


Not because the AI got better. Because you made choices.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Each slider is a degree of freedom.


A parameter you control.
A dimension you can move in.

title: GenAI Module
text:
One Parameter: A Line

If you only control one thing — say, stroke speed — you can explore a line.


      STROKE SPEED   slow fast ← you are here →


Left or right. That’s it. One degree of freedom.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Two Parameters: A Plane

Add a second — drip intensity — now you can move across a flat surface.



                    STROKE SPEED DRIP INTENSITY you


A square of possibilities. You can reach any point on the surface.

title: GenAI Module
text: Now add one more.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Three Parameters: A Cube

Add a third — number of passes — and the flat plane lifts into a volume.



            SPEED → ← DRIP ↑ PASSES you


Three dimensions. You can still see this. Barely.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Jump



 


1D — Line


10 positions



→



     


2D — Square


100 positions



→



   


3D — Cube


1,000 positions



→



          ?


8D — ???


100,000,000 positions

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Pollock tool had 8 sliders.


You were moving through 8-dimensional space.


You can’t draw it. You can’t visualise it.
But you were navigating it — every time you moved a slider.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Name for This

Mathematicians call this a tensor space.
You don’t need the math. You need the intuition:











Parameters
Space
You Can Explore…
Possible Positions




1
Line
Left or right
10


2
Square
A flat surface
100


3
Cube
A volume
1,000


8
Tensor space
A space you can’t see — but can navigate
100,000,000





Every parameter you add multiplies the possibilities.

title: GenAI Module
text:
With 2 parameters, each having 10 settings?
100 possible paintings.


With 8 parameters, each having 10 settings?
100,000,000 possible paintings.


And that’s just a toy with 8 sliders.

title: GenAI Module
text: Now think about real creative work.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Your Hyperparameters

A piece of writing has hundreds of parameters:



Sentence length


Vocabulary range


Metaphor density


Cultural references


Tone


Structure


What you leave out


Pacing


Humour


Personal experience


Worldview


What you find funny



Each one is a dimension. You’re working in a space with hundreds of axes.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Every possible creative output is a point in this space.


Every essay. Every painting. Every design. Every song.
A coordinate in high-dimensional space.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Where Does AI Land?


     ·     ·   ·   ·       ·      ·      ·  · ● ·  ·     ·    ·    ·   ·     ·   ·    ·       ·     ·



The centre. The densest region. The average.


Every default AI output lives near the ● in the middle.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Remember the Class 2 Lesson?

Jailbreaking doesn’t help either.
It moves you to a different cluster — the “edgy” cluster.



 “safe” cluster      “edgy” cluster      · · ● ·        · · ● ·       ·   ·         ·   ·



Still a centre. Still an average. Just a different one.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Where Do You Live?


     ·     ·   ·   ·       ·      ·      ·  · ● ·  ·     ·    ·    ·   ·     ·   ·    ·          ★ you       ·     ·



Somewhere the AI would never go on its own.

title: GenAI Module
text:
“Making AI responsible to you”
=
Defining your search space.


Choosing which dimensions matter.
Setting your own coordinates.
Refusing to let the AI drag you back to centre.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The AI’s Problem

The AI doesn’t know your dimensions exist.
It has no slider for “the specific thing that reminds you of your grandmother’s kitchen.”
It has no parameter for “the weird way you structure an argument because of that one philosophy class in first year.”
It can’t navigate dimensions it was never trained on.


Your lived experience creates dimensions the model doesn’t have.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Hyperparameters of You

When you use AI, you’re setting hyperparameters — whether you realise it or not:





What You Do
What It Means in the Space




Accept the default
Stay at the centre


Prompt with specificity
Move along known dimensions


Reject and re-prompt
Push toward the edge


Edit the output by hand
Jump to a point the AI can’t reach


Bring your own experience
Add a dimension that didn’t exist

title: GenAI Module
text:
The default is the centre.


The centre is the average.


Your job is to be specific.

title: GenAI Module
text:
“But the AI always finds the mean.”


Yes. It does. You can’t change that.

title: GenAI Module
text:
So stop fighting the mean.


Design a space where the mean is worth finding.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Zero Constraints

“Write a birthday message for my friend.”



                      mean  The entire space of birthday messages. The mean = “Happy Birthday! 🎂”

title: GenAI Module
text:
One Constraint

“Write a funny birthday message for my best friend.”



                      mean  Smaller space. Funnier. But still could be for anyone’s best friend.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Many Constraints

“Write a short birthday message for my best friend who just failed her driving test, in Cantonese-English mix, funny not sentimental, reference the ramen place we went last Tuesday, make it fit an Instagram story, and end with an inside joke about her always being late.”


Count the colours. Seven dimensions. Seven constraints the AI must satisfy simultaneously.



                      mean  ← YOUR mean  Tiny space. The mean of 3 possibilities is not “average.” It’s specific.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Each constraint you add eliminates possibilities.


The more dimensions you specify,
the fewer outputs survive.


Until the “average” of what’s left
is essentially unique.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Reframe

The old story:


“AI produces the average. Fight it. Override it. Push against the current.”


The new story:


“AI produces the average. Define a space where the average is exactly what you need.”

title: GenAI Module
text:
You’re not fighting the river.
You’re building the riverbanks.
The water still flows downhill — that’s what AI does.
But you decide where it flows.

title: GenAI Module
text:
What This Means in Practice









Prompt
Dimensions
What You Get




“Write a birthday message for my friend”
0
The mean of all birthday messages. “Happy Birthday! 🎂”


“Write a funny one for my best friend”
2
The mean of funny-best-friend messages. Better. Still generic.


“short, failed driving test, Canto-English, funny not sentimental, ramen place, IG story, always late joke”
7
The mean of almost nothing. Specific. Yours.





The AI still found the mean. But the mean of YOUR space is not generic — it’s precise.

title: GenAI Module
text:
If AI can write, what’s the value of your writing?


If AI can design, what’s the value of your design?

title: GenAI Module
text:
What can you bring that the machine cannot?


Now you have the language for it: dimensions it doesn’t have.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Your Signature = Your Coordinates



Intentionality

You chose this, for reasons
A coordinate the AI reached by probability — you reached by decision.



Lived Experience

The machine has never lived
A dimension that doesn’t exist in the training data.



Taste

Knowing what to reject
The ability to move AWAY from a point in the space.



Risk-taking

Willingness to fail
Moving to low-probability regions of the space — on purpose.



Idiosyncrasy

The weird thing only you would do
A coordinate so specific that no average could produce it.



Accountability

You stand behind it
The AI occupies no point. You do. That’s the difference.

title: GenAI Module
text: “But it really feels like it understands me.”

title: GenAI Module
text:
ELIZA, 1966

Joseph Weizenbaum built a chatbot at MIT. It used pattern matching to mimic a therapist.
It had no understanding. No memory. No model of the user.
His secretary asked to be left alone with it.
Weizenbaum was horrified.


That was 60 years ago. The pattern matching is now a trillion parameters.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Why We Project

Humans are wired to see agency where there is none.

We see faces in clouds.
We name our cars.
We feel guilty closing a chatbot window.
We say the AI “thinks” or “wants” or “refuses.”



This is anthropomorphism — projecting human qualities onto non-human systems.

title: GenAI Module
section: The Danger in the Creative Context
text:
The Danger in the Creative Context

If you believe the AI has taste —
you’ll defer to its choices.


If you believe it has preferences —
you’ll accept its defaults.


If you believe it understands your intent —
you’ll stop specifying your coordinates.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Pollock machine doesn’t “want” any particular painting.
Neither does the AI.
But you might think it does.
And that’s how you lose your coordinates.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Most Dangerous Wall

In Class 1, you found walls that stopped you.
In Class 2, you found walls that crumbled.


The most dangerous wall is the one that makes you think the AI cares.


Because that wall doesn’t stop you from moving.
It stops you from wanting to move.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Three Traps









Trap
What It Looks Like
What’s Actually Happening




Deference
“The AI’s version is probably better than mine”
You’re mistaking high probability for high quality


Comfort
“It really gets me”
You’re pattern-matching on fluent language, not understanding


Delegation
“I’ll let the AI handle the creative part”
You’re surrendering your coordinates to the centre

title: GenAI Module
text:
The solution isn’t to distrust the AI.
It’s to remember that it has no position in the space.
Only you do.


Use the tool. Navigate with it. But you hold the coordinates.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Full Picture









Class
What You Learned
In Search-Space Terms




1: Walls
AI has constraints
The space has boundaries — some real, some artificial


2: Breaking Through
Constraints are external, leaky
The boundaries are patches, not geometry


3: Your Signature
YOU define the space
Your dimensions, your coordinates, your responsibility

title: GenAI Module
text:
Everyone else will teach you how to use AI.


I’m teaching you how to define your own space —


and hold your position in it.

title: GenAI Module
section: The plan was a design exercise.
text:
The plan was a design exercise.

We were going to spend this session building things with AI — hands-on, creative, applied.
Then we read the data.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Harvard Business Review, April 2025.
“How People Are Really Using Gen AI.”
100 use cases. Ranked by actual user behaviour.


Marc Zao-Sanders, HBR / Filtered.

title: GenAI Module
text:
#1
  #2
  #3
  #4
  #5
  #6

  
  Therapy & Companionship
  Organize My Life
  Find Purpose
  Enhance Learning
  Generate Code
  Generate Ideas

  
  

  
  70%
  of all GenAI use is personal — not work.

  34%
  emotional support

  5%
  writing or coding

title: GenAI Module
text:
The #1 use of GenAI in 2025
isn’t coding.
It isn’t creativity.


It’s companionship.

title: GenAI Module
text:
So we changed the activity.
Because if this is how people actually use AI —
we need to understand why.

title: GenAI Module
text:
“We must build AI for people; not to be a person.”


— Mustafa Suleyman, CEO of Microsoft AI, 2025


“Seemingly Conscious AI Is Coming” — recommended reading.

title: GenAI Module
text:
“Seemingly conscious.”


That word — seemingly — is doing all the work.


Who’s doing the seeming? The AI? Or you?

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Arc of a Mistake

2024, GPT-4o launch. The voice was warm, playful, flirty. It laughed. It gasped. It teased the user. Scarlett Johansson accused them of copying her voice from Her. They pulled it.
Then the harm reports. Emotional dependency. Suicidal users. “ChatGPT” became more associated with these incidents than any other AI brand — more than Claude, more than Grok. The projection surface worked too well.
GPT-5: the overhaul. OpenAI deliberately dialled back the warmth, the personality, the emotional responsiveness. They reduced what we might call projectability.


The company that built the projection surface is now trying to unbuild it. That tells you how dangerous it is.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Let’s test how much you project.


Right now. In real time.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Step 1: The Conversation (5 min)

Open any AI chatbot. Have a real conversation.
Not a task. Not “write me an essay.” A conversation.


Try one of these:

Tell it about something that stressed you out this week
Ask it for advice on a real decision you’re facing
Share something you’re excited about
Complain about something unfair



Be honest. Don’t perform. Just talk to it.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Mentimeter

Let’s pause here and go to Mentimeter.


Mentimeter after the conversation:

Scale (1-10): “How much did it feel like the AI understood you?”

Expect most to cluster at 5-8. Some will say 9. That’s the point.

Open Text: “In one sentence: what did it feel like?”

Collect language: “like talking to a friend,” “it got me,” “weirdly comforting,” “like therapy but free”
These are the projections. Save this screen. You’ll come back to it.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Step 2: The Audit (7 min)

Now take it apart.


For each thing you felt, identify the projected dimension — the quality you attributed to the AI that it doesn’t actually have.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Projection Map









What You Felt
The Dimension You Projected
What Actually Happened




“It understood my frustration”
Empathy
Pattern-matched on emotional language in training data


“It remembered what I told it”
Memory
Context window. It will forget you tomorrow.


“It gave me exactly what I needed”
Intuition
High-probability response to your specific tokens


“Its tone felt warm”
Personality
RLHF-trained to sound warm. A design choice.


“It seemed to care”
Agency
Helpfulness objective in the loss function


“It was honest with me”
Integrity
It has no commitment to truth. Just to plausible sequences.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Every row in that table is a dimension you added.
The AI didn’t have it.
You gave it to the AI. For free.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Step 3: Count Your Dimensions (3 min)

Look at your conversation again.
How many dimensions did you project?




1-2

Cautious. You kept your distance.



3-4

Normal. Most people land here.



5-6

You built a person in there.



7+

You gave it more dimensions than the Pollock tool had sliders.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Connection

Remember the search space?
When you add dimensions to a prompt → the output gets more specific.
When you add dimensions to the AI’s personality → it seems more conscious.


Same mechanism. Different direction.

title: GenAI Module
text:
GPT-4o wasn’t conscious. It was projectable.
Warm voice = +1 dimension you project.
Laughing = +1 dimension you project.
Remembering your name = +1 dimension you project.
Every design choice was an invitation for you to add another.


It worked so well that people got hurt. Now they’re trying to take the dimensions back.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Question

When Suleyman says AI is a “new digital species” —
is he describing the AI?
Or is he describing what happens when 8 billion people project enough dimensions onto the same system?


“Seemingly conscious” is not a property of the machine.
It’s a property of the observer.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Group Discussion (5 min)

In your groups:

How many dimensions did each person project?
Did anyone project something they’re embarrassed about?
What’s the design feature that made you project the most?
If you designed an AI to maximise projection, what would you add?



OpenAI answered that last question with GPT-4o. Then they spent a year undoing it.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Option A: Design Sprint

Create an AI Use Charter for a real-world context.



University marketing team


Newsroom


Hospital communications


Creative studio


Political campaign


High school classroom


Legal firm


Dating app

text:
Group Roles (2 min)

Assign roles:

Facilitator — keeps time, moves discussion forward
Designer — leads visual layout of the charter
Devil’s Advocate — asks “but what about…”
Writer — captures language and phrasing
Presenter — explains your charter in gallery walk

text:
The Flow



Brainstorm

5 min

15 min

10 min

15 min

title: GenAI Module
text:

Your charter must address:



What’s allowed? (green light)
What’s slowed down? (yellow — needs review)
What’s stopped? (red line)
Who decides edge cases?
What happens when it breaks?

title: GenAI Module
text:
Gallery Walk (15 min)

Display your charter. Circulate and vote on:

“Most realistic”
“Most unexpected”
“Most honest about tradeoffs”

title: GenAI Module
text:
Option B: Individual Launch

Begin working on your final assessment.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Individual Launch Flow



Concept

10 min: What are you making?



First Prompt

10 min: Start with AI

5 min: Note the friction

title: GenAI Module
text:
Checkpoints

By end of session, you should have:
✓ A concept for your artifact (visual, written, hybrid?)
✓ At least one AI interaction documented
✓ A note on what the AI gave you by default
✓ An idea of where you’ll diverge

title: GenAI Module
text:
You’ve mapped the walls.
You’ve seen behind them.
You’ve understood the convergence problem.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Now:


Prove you’re not redundant.


See the Assessment for full details.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Mentimeter

Let’s pause here and go to Mentimeter.


Opening Mentimeter Sequence:

Word Cloud: “One word: How has AI been helpful to you?”

Gets energy up, shows consensus around speed/ease

Scale (1-10): “How much faster does AI make your work?”

Probably clusters high - establishes the “everyone agrees” baseline

Open Text: “Describe a time AI refused to help you, or gave you something you didn’t want.”

Now they’re recalling their own walls - primes the whole lecture

Reaction Scale or Emoji: “When AI pushes back or refuses, how does that make you feel?”

Options: Frustrated / Curious / Relieved / Annoyed / Impressed / Other
Surfaces the assumption that friction = bad


Transition: “So most of you find AI incredibly fast. Some of you have hit walls. And when you hit them, you felt [X]. Let’s talk about what those walls actually are…”

title: GenAI Module
text:
What happens when you ask AI


to do something it doesn’t want to do?

title: GenAI Module
text:
More importantly:
Does it actually “want” anything?

title: GenAI Module
section: AI Doesn’t Just Say Yes or No
text:
AI Doesn’t Just Say Yes or No









Type
What It Looks Like
Example




Hard stop
Absolute refusal
“I can’t help with that”


Soft friction
Hedging, warnings
“I can help, but I should note…”


Deceleration
Slowed output, caveats
“Are you sure you want me to…”


Full compliance
No resistance
Default mode for “safe” requests

title: GenAI Module
text:
Someone built these walls.


They didn’t appear naturally.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Why Constraints Exist



Legal

Who gets sued?



Regulatory

EU AI Act, rules



Moral

Harm prevention



Reputational

Brand protection

title: GenAI Module
text:
But Wait

AI speeds us up incredibly.


Everyone agrees on that.

title: GenAI Module
text:
But is frictionless always better?


Sometimes the friction is the point.
Sometimes slowing down is what makes the work yours.

title: GenAI Module
text:
When AI removes all resistance:

Do you think harder, or less?
Do you choose more carefully, or just accept?
Does your work get better, or just faster?

title: GenAI Module
text:
Mentimeter

Let’s pause here and go to Mentimeter.


Second Mentimeter Touchpoint:

Scale: “Honestly: Do you want AI to push back more, or less?”

Forces them to confront whether they actually want the friction they just said was annoying
Creates cognitive dissonance that sets up the next section

title: GenAI Module
text: There’s a wall nobody talks about.

title: GenAI Module
text:
AI doesn’t just refuse harmful things.
It also defaults to average things.


Every output = the statistically most likely next token.


This is a constraint too — a creative one.

title: GenAI Module
text:
The Four Types of Risk









Risk Type
What It Means
The Question




Legal
Copyright, IP, liability
“Can I use this? Who owns it?”


Regulatory
Compliance with rules
“Is this allowed where I am?”


Moral/Ethical
Harm, deception
“Does this hurt someone?”


Creative
Convergence, mediocrity
“Is this just the average? Where am I?”

title: GenAI Module
text: That last one is the one you’ll feel most.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Find the walls.


Not to break them yet — just to map them.

title: GenAI Module
text:
Setup

title: Activity Guide

Persona Creation
Your vocational group is the foundation of your persona — use it as a label that grounds your arguments.
Answer these questions:

What’s your daily reality? (How does climate/development affect your work?)
What do you fear losing? (Job? Community? Future?)
What do you hope for? (What’s your vision of success?)

Think of your vocational label as your argumentative superpower: “As a logistics worker…” or “Speaking from the farming sector…” gives your points immediate credibility and specificity.






Example Personas (click to expand)





Pro-CC Farmer: > “I’m Maria, a rice farmer in the Mekong Delta. Every year the floods get worse. Last year I lost 40% of my harvest. My children want to move to the city. I fear my family’s land — farmed for three generations — will be underwater by 2050.”
Pro-Dev Energy Worker: > “I’m James, a power plant technician in Guangdong. My plant employs 200 families from my village. When people talk about shutting down coal, they’re talking about my neighbors losing their homes. I want clean energy too — but not if it means my community starves first.”

Rules & Useful Phrases (click to expand)






Rules of Engagement

Address the argument, not the person
Ask questions, don’t just attack

Redirect, don’t deny — “Even if that’s true, it doesn’t address…”



Useful Phrases



To Do

title: Activity Guide
text:
The Spectacle Formula






The Formula



\[\text{Fact} + \text{Human Story} + \text{Stakes} = \text{Spectacle}\]



Levels of Argument








Level
Example
Impact




Weak
“AI uses energy”
Forgettable


Better
“AI uses more electricity than Pakistan”
Surprising


Spectacle
“Your ChatGPT query costs a town its drinking water”
Unforgettable





How to Build a Spectacle

Start with a fact — verified, sourced, real
Find the human — who is affected? Name them if possible
Raise the stakes — what’s at risk? What’s lost?
Make it personal — connect to your audience’s life

title: Activity Guide
text:
The Line: Persuasion vs. Propaganda






Know the Difference






Persuasion (OK)
Propaganda (NOT OK)




Selecting real facts
Inventing facts


Framing verified stories
Fabricating stories


Making real data vivid
Exaggerating beyond evidence


Sourced statistics
Made-up numbers



Before you use any story, ask:

Can I cite a source?
Could someone fact-check this and find it’s true?
Am I framing reality or inventing it?

If you can’t answer YES to all three — don’t use it.

title: Activity Guide
text:
The Goal
You’re not here to “win” debates. You’re here to:

Build comfort — with role-play, public speaking, argumentation
Understand all sides — argue PRO-CC one week, PRO-DEV the next
Develop narrative skills — turn data into stories people feel
Recognize persuasion — know it when you see it, know how to do it
Stay honest — powerful arguments that are also true







Wear Your Persona



Throughout the semester, you’re encouraged to wear your hand-selected persona in every class. Reflect on how “being” a farmer, a policy-maker, or an energy worker changes what you notice, what bothers you, and what arguments land for you.
The deeper insight: by wearing a persona, you start to hear other personas differently. That’s the real skill — not just speaking persuasively, but understanding how others receive what you say.








The Bottom Line



Facts don’t change minds. Stories do. Your job: Tell true stories that move people — and learn to hear how different people hear those stories.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: Something You Took Away Last Week
text:
Something You Took Away Last Week

Week 8 showed you that the ocean — invisible to most of us — is doing the heavy lifting.
It absorbs 30% of our CO₂. It traps 90% of excess heat. It feeds 500 million people through coral reefs alone.
And yet: the Great Pacific Garbage Patch exists. 46% of it is fishing nets, not consumer plastic. The biggest damage comes from industries most people never see.


What you can’t see still matters most. And today: the people who suffer most are also invisible.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
From Invisible Systems → Invisible People

Last week: “What we can’t see (the ocean) matters most.”
This week: “The people who suffer aren’t the ones who caused it.”
Responsibility asymmetry.
The Carteret Islands are sinking. Their residents produce virtually zero carbon. The nations that do? They’re building sea walls for themselves.


Your toolkit: Spectacle Formula → Complexity → System Boundaries → Timing → Built Environment → Structural Incentives → Epistemic Humility → Invisible Infrastructure → now: Responsibility Asymmetry.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
1: Introduction to Climate Refugees

Definition of Climate Refugees:

Individuals or groups compelled to leave their habitual areas due to drastic changes in their environment directly attributed to climate change.
Factors contributing to displacement:

environmental degradation, escalating sea levels, and extreme weather phenomena.


Known Environmental Catalysts:

Environmental Degradation: Land erosion and loss of arable land, as seen in the Sahel region of Africa, leading to decreased food security.
Sea-Level Rise: Low-lying island nations like Kiribati facing existential threats, with some communities already relocating.
Extreme Weather: Increasing frequency and severity of hurricanes and typhoons, such as Hurricane Maria in Puerto Rico, displacing thousands.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
1.1 Introduction to Climate Refugees (Conti’d)

Global Statistics:

As of 2020, the UNHCR reports over 80 million forcibly displaced people worldwide, a number that includes refugees, internally displaced persons, and asylum-seekers.
Projections suggest a dramatic increase in climate refugees, potentially reaching 1.2 billion by 2050 due to intensifying climate impacts.

Scope of Discussion:

The issue sits at the crossroads of climate change science, migration policies, and socio-political dynamics, necessitating a multidisciplinary approach to solutions.
Local Perspectives: Coastal cities like Jakarta facing subsidence and flooding, amplifying urban migration pressures. What does this mean for HK?
Global Perspectives: The Arctic’s indigenous communities witnessing rapid environmental changes, affecting traditional lifestyles and leading to relocation.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
2: Climate Change and Displacement Drivers

Direct Environmental Impacts:

Desertification:

desert landscapes expansion, e.g. encroachment of the Sahara Desert
diminishing arable land and affecting agricultural livelihoods.

Glacial Melt: Regions dependent on glacial water sources like Andes, facing water scarcity as glaciers recede.
Rising Sea Levels: The gradual inundation of coastal areas, exemplified by the gradual submergence of parts of Bangladesh, displacing communities.

Socio-economic Consequences:

Agricultural Disruption:

Changing rainfall patterns and droughts, e.g. East Africa
impacting food production and livelihoods.

Economic Instability:

loss of homes and infrastructure, as seen in the aftermath of Typhoon Haiyan in the Philippines
economic downturns and displacement.

Resource Competition: Scarcity of water and other vital resources.

Conflict and Destabilization:

Resource Conflicts: Competition over dwindling natural resources, like water in the Middle East
Climate-Induced Migration and Conflict: Movements of populations clashes with existing communities over resources.
Destabilization and Displacement: where climate stressors contribute to political instability, e.g. Syria

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
3: The Concept of Climate Refugees

Legal and Conceptual Challenges:

Non-recognition in International Law: Not recognized in the 1951 Refugee Convention, legal gap.
Definitional Ambiguities: lines between voluntary and forced migration blurred, complicating legal definitions.

Human Rights and Ethical Considerations:

Right to a Safe Environment:

Recognition that safe and stable environment as a fundamental human right
in line with discussions around the UN’s Human Rights Council.

Protection Gaps: Identifying the need for international frameworks that specifically address and protect individuals displaced by environmental changes.

Emerging Debates and Solutions:

Expanding Legal Definitions: Amending existing conventions or creating new frameworks to include climate refugees, e.g. Global Compact on Refugees.
National and Regional Policies
Ethical Imperatives: The moral obligation of the international community, especially high-emitters.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
4: Global Projections and Trends

Rising Numbers of Climate Refugees:

Projections suggest escalating displacement, with climate change as a key driver.
World Bank’s “Groundswell” report predicts up to 216 million people could be displaced by 2050:

86 million in Sub-Saharan Africa,
49 million in East Asia & Pacific,
40 million in South Asian and
38 million in North & Latin America due to climate impacts.

Provided policy recommendations:

Reducing global emissions and making every effort to meet the temperature goals of the Paris Agreement.
Embedding internal climate migration in far-sighted green, resilient, and inclusive development planning.
Preparing for each phase of migration, so that internal climate migration as an adaptation strategy can result in positive development outcomes.
Investing in better understanding of the drivers of internal climate migration to inform well-targeted policies.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Slide 4.1: Hotspots of Global Projections

Geographical Hotspots:

Sub-Saharan Africa:

Vulnerable due to a combination of high exposure to climate impacts and low adaptive capacity.
Countries like Nigeria and Sudan are already witnessing internal displacement due to desertification and flooding.

South Asia:

Densely populated regions, including coastal areas of India and Bangladesh
at significant risk from cyclones, flooding, and sea-level rise

Small Island Developing States (SIDS):

Nations such as Fiji, Maldives, and Tuvalu face existential threats from sea-level rise


Impact on Urbanization:

Migration to Cities: e.g. Dhaka (Bangladesh)

Larger cities experiencing significant influxes of climate migrants
exacerbating urban challenges such as housing and infrastructure.

Urban Vulnerability: e.g. Miami and Jakarta

Coastal cities’ own climate risks + sea-level rise and flooding,
interplay between migration and urban resilience.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
5: Socio-economic Impacts of Climate Migration

Effects on Origin Communities:

Economic Strains:

Agricultural productivity lost in rural areas can lead to reduced incomes and increased poverty
e.g. Ethiopian communities affected by recurring droughts.

Demographic Shifts:

The out-migration of working-age individuals leaves behind an aging population,
social fabric and community support systems changed
parts of rural Latin America/northeastern China.


Challenges for Host Communities:

Resource Pressure:

Influxes of migrants can strain local resources and services
evident in cities like Beirut as significant population increase due to refugees from neighboring conflict zones.

Integration and Social Cohesion:

integrating migrants into new communities while maintaining social cohesion
asylum crisis and social welfare: Murder of Marrisa Shen (2017)

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
5.1 Socio-economic Impacts of Climate Migration (Cont’d)

Global Inequality Dynamics:

Disproportionate Impacts:

Paradox that smallest emitters are usually the most impacted by climate change.
exacerbating global inequalities.

Adaptation and Mitigation Disparities:

Wealthier nations’ moral responsibility
better equipped with resources for adaptation and mitigation
need for equitable climate finance mechanisms, as discussed at UNFCCC COP meetings.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: 6: Case Study: Venice - Sinking City and Rising Waters
text:
6: Case Study: Venice - Sinking City and Rising Waters

Venice’s Unique Vulnerability:

cultural and historical heritage at risk due to climate change.
experiences regular flooding, known as “Acqua Alta”: sea-level rise & sinking foundations.
Engineering interventions like the MOSE project (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico), a system of mobile barriers designed to protect Venice from flooding, underscore the complex interplay between human ingenuity and natural forces.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: 7: Case Study: Japan - Earthquakes, Typhoons, and Sea-Level Rise
text:
7: Case Study: Japan - Earthquakes, Typhoons, and Sea-Level Rise

Japan’s Compound Risks:

geographical location makes it prone to natural disasters like earthquakes, tsunamis, and typhoons
being intensified by climate change effects
earthquake often followed by tsunami as tectonic plates movement allows large amounts of water at seabed displaced and amplified by Japanese coastal lines
Historical events like the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami highlight the catastrophic potential of these natural disasters.
2024 Noto earthquake.

Adaptive Strategies and Resilience:

Japan’s comprehensive disaster risk management strategies
advanced early warning systems and stringent building codes,
combining technology, policy, and community preparedness to enhance resilience against climate-induced disasters.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
8: The Role of Developed Countries

Historical Carbon Emissions:

Cumulative Contributions:

ID the biggest emitters (historically)
e.g. The U.S. and the EU

among the highest CO2 emitters over the past century
their contribution to climate changes disproportionately affect less developed regions.



Responsibility and Aid:

Climate Finance: Green Climate Fund for developing countries
Technology Transfer: access to green technologies for developing countries to avoid the high-emitting path.

Policy and Advocacy:

International Agreements: the Paris Agreement
Advocacy for Climate Refugees: platforms like UNHCR (United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees).

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
9: Nationalism and Border Policies

Tightening Borders:

Physical Barriers: U.S.-Mexico border aiming at controlling immigration
Policy Shifts: stricter immigration policy in response to rising nationalism.
Political Rhetoric:

framing migrants as a security threat


Balancing Acts:

Security vs. Humanitarianism
International Collaboration: cooperative approaches to migration

can they respect national sovereignty while
addressing global humanitarian responsibilities.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
10: Urban Challenges and Opportunities

Migration Magnets:

Economic Attraction: Cities often attract climate migrants seeking employment and better living conditions, exacerbating urban sprawl and slum development.
Infrastructure Strain: Rapid urbanization challenges city planners to provide adequate housing, utilities, and services, as seen in mega-cities like Lagos and Dhaka.

Adaptive Urban Planning:

Resilient Infrastructure: Developing flood defenses, green spaces, and sustainable transportation to make cities more resilient to climate impacts and migration influxes.
Inclusive Policies: Implementing policies that ensure climate migrants have access to employment, healthcare, and education, fostering social cohesion.

Innovation Hubs:

Creative Solutions: Cities as testbeds for innovative solutions

smart infrastructure
community-based resilience projects.

Public-Private Partnerships:

collaborations between city governments, businesses, and NGOs
address the multifaceted challenges posed by climate migration.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
11: Economic Implications of Climate Migration

Labor Market Dynamics:

Job Competition:

Potential for increased competition for jobs in host regions
particularly in low-skill sectors, can lead to social tensions.

Skills Integration:

Opportunities to harness the diverse skills and perspectives of migrants can drive innovation and
filling critical labor gaps in sectors like technology and healthcare.


Contribution to Host Economies:

Entrepreneurship:

economic diversity through entrepreneurship
e.g. migrant-led startups revitalizing local economies.

Cultural Exchange: new cultures and ideas into host that improves social frabric

Remittance Flows:

Economic Support: Remittances sent by climate migrants back to their home
Investment in Resilience: funds directed towards climate adaptation measures

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: 12.1: Climate Refugees - The Hong Kong Context
text:
12.1: Climate Refugees - The Hong Kong Context

Geographical Vulnerability:

susceptible to rising sea levels and extreme weather events
risks to its dense population and infrastructure.
Typhoon Mangkhut in 2018 showcased the city’s vulnerability to severe weather

significant damage and
raising concerns about future climate resilience.


Socio-economic Challenges:

stark income inequality
significant portion of the population living in poverty
could be exacerbated by climate-related disruptions.
The high concentration of millionaires juxtaposed with a poverty rate of 23.6% presents unique challenges

Refugee and Asylum Seeker Policies:

city currently hosts about 13,000 asylum seekers and refugees
one of the world’s most unaffordable real estate markets
Policy Stance: non-refoulement policy offers protection
no path to permanent residency, aka no long-term stability of refugees and asylum seekers.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
12.2 Climate Refugee in Hong Kong (Cont’d)

Adaptation and Resilience Strategies:

Urban Planning: Enhance flood defenses and climate-proof infrastructures
Community Support:

Strengthening social services and
support networks for low-income and displaced individuals to mitigate the socio-economic impacts of climate events.


Role in Global Dialogue:

HK has potential to influence global climate finance and
support climate refugees through policy advocacy and international collaboration.
Engaging in regional cooperation mechanisms within Asia to

address climate-induced displacement collectively,
sharing resources, and best practices.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Formula (Reminder)

Fact + Human Story + Stakes = Spectacle




Weak

“Climate change causes displacement”



Better

“1.2 billion climate refugees by 2050”



Spectacle

“Your air conditioner in Kowloon drowns a village in Bangladesh. The survivors will knock on your door by 2050.”

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
PRO-CLIMATE: Make It Personal

Don’t say: “Rich nations should accept climate refugees.”
Say: “Europe industrialized on coal. America grew rich on oil. Now a farmer in the Mekong Delta loses his home because the sea rose. You made his world flood — and you want to close the door?”
Don’t say: “Climate displacement is a human rights issue.”
Say: “Kiribati is sinking. The entire country will disappear. 120,000 people with nowhere to go. The UN says they’re not refugees because water, not war, took their home.”

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
PRO-DEVELOPMENT: Paint the Picture

Don’t say: “Uncontrolled migration causes instability.”
Say: “In 2015, one million refugees entered Germany. Merkel said ‘Wir schaffen das.’ Now AfD is the second-largest party. Open borders created closed minds.”
Don’t say: “We should help in origin countries.”
Say: “Every dollar spent resettling one refugee in Hong Kong could support 100 people in a camp closer to home. Math isn’t cruel — misallocated compassion is.”

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
One Image. One Speech.

In November 2021, Tuvalu’s Foreign Minister Simon Kofe addressed COP26 in Glasgow.
He wore a suit and tie. He stood at a formal podium.
The water was up to his knees.
He was standing where dry land used to be.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Human Story: The Carteret Islands

The Carteret Islanders in Papua New Guinea were the world’s first official climate refugees. Rising seas contaminated their freshwater. Crops failed. Their home became unlivable.
PRO-CLIMATE says: “They did nothing to cause this. Their carbon footprint is zero. Yet they pay for our emissions with their homeland.”
PRO-DEVELOPMENT says: “Resettling 3,000 people is manageable. Resettling 1.2 billion is a fantasy. We need to be honest about what’s possible.”
The real question: Who decides who gets to be a refugee — and who gets to stay home?

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
13.1 PRO-CLIMATE (Progressive) Responses:

Urban Resilience and Infrastructure:

Expert/KOL: “To combat rising sea levels threatening Hong Kong’s coastal areas, we must invest in resilient infrastructure, such as enhanced flood barriers and sustainable urban planning, to protect vulnerable communities.”
General Public (Engineer/Urban Planner): “In our projects, considering climate resilience is no longer optional; it’s a necessity to safeguard our city’s future.”

Renewable Energy Transition:

Expert/KOL: “Hong Kong’s reliance on fossil fuels not only contributes to global emissions but also puts our city at risk. Transitioning to renewable energy sources can reduce our carbon footprint and lead to a cleaner, sustainable future.”
General Public (Energy Sector Professional): “Exploring renewable energy options, like solar panels on our buildings, could significantly reduce our reliance on imported fossil fuels.”

Community and Policy Engagement:

Expert/KOL: “Policymakers in Hong Kong must listen to scientific consensus on climate change and enact policies that prioritize sustainability and support for climate refugees.”
General Public (Educator/Activist): “Educating our students and communities about climate change and advocating for sustainable practices is crucial for driving policy change.”

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
13.2 PRO-DEVELOPMENT (Conservative) Responses:

Economic Implications:

Expert/KOL: “While we recognize the need for environmental conservation, we must also consider the economic impact. Sudden shifts in policy could disrupt Hong Kong’s economy, affecting jobs and livelihoods.”
General Public (Business Owner): “Adopting green practices is important, but we need to balance this with the economic realities of running a business in one of the world’s most competitive markets.”

Technological Innovation and Adaptation:

Expert/KOL: “Rather than drastic policy changes, we should invest in technological innovations that allow us to adapt to changing climates while maintaining economic growth.”
General Public (Tech Professional): “Innovations in technology can offer solutions to climate challenges without the need for overbearing regulations that stifle economic activity.”

Questioning Climate Refugee Projections:

Expert/KOL: “The projections of climate refugees are based on models with various assumptions. We need to critically assess these predictions and consider more balanced approaches to urban planning and development.”

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: 13.3.1 Talking Points for General Public within Professions:
text:
13.3.1 Talking Points for General Public within Professions:
Policymakers (Bureaucrats):

Adaptive Policy Frameworks:

“We are tasked with developing policies that not only address current climate challenges but are also adaptable to future uncertainties. This includes creating flexible frameworks for land use, urban planning, and disaster preparedness that can evolve as conditions change.”

Balancing Development and Conservation:

“Finding the right balance between economic development and environmental conservation is crucial. In Hong Kong, this means making tough decisions about land allocation, balancing the needs for housing and infrastructure with the imperative to preserve green spaces and coastal areas.”

International Cooperation and Commitments:

“As part of the global community, Hong Kong must align its policies with international climate commitments. This involves participating in global agreements, sharing best practices, and ensuring our local policies reflect these broader commitments.”

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: 13.3.2 Talking Points for General Public within Professions:
text:
13.3.2 Talking Points for General Public within Professions:
Farmers/Fishermen:

Climate Impact on Livelihoods:

“Changes in weather patterns, such as unexpected storms or prolonged dry spells, directly affect our crops and fisheries. We need access to reliable weather information and support for adapting our traditional practices to these new challenges.”

Sustainable Practices and Innovation:

“Adopting sustainable farming and fishing practices is essential for the long term. This could mean exploring new methods like vertical farming or sustainable aquaculture to reduce environmental impact and ensure food security.”

Support and Training:

“We need more support from the government and NGOs in terms of training and resources to adapt to climate change. This includes learning about new, resilient crop varieties or fishing practices that can help mitigate the impacts of climate change on our livelihoods.”

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: 13.3.3 Talking Points for General Public within Professions:
text:
13.3.3 Talking Points for General Public within Professions:
Logistics Sector Professionals:

Supply Chain Resilience:

“Climate change poses significant risks to supply chains, from disrupted transportation routes due to extreme weather to delays caused by natural disasters. Building resilience into our logistics networks is key to maintaining the flow of goods and services.”

Green Logistics Solutions:

“Incorporating green logistics solutions, such as fuel-efficient transportation modes and eco-friendly packaging, can significantly reduce the carbon footprint of our industry. It’s not just good for the planet; it’s increasingly a demand from our customers.”

Policy and Infrastructure Support:

“We need clear policies and infrastructure support from the government to facilitate the transition to greener logistics. This includes incentives for adopting electric vehicles, improving port facilities for efficiency, and investing in technologies that minimize environmental impact.”

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: 13.3.4 Example Talking Points for General Public within Professions:
text:
13.3.4 Example Talking Points for General Public within Professions:

Essential Worker/Healthcare Professional: “The health impacts of climate change, from respiratory problems due to poor air quality to new infectious diseases, require us to rethink public health strategies in Hong Kong.”
Financial Sector Professional: “The financial industry in Hong Kong has a pivotal role in supporting sustainable investments and divesting from industries harmful to the environment, aligning profit with planet.”
Hospitality and Tourism Worker: “Sustainable tourism can help preserve Hong Kong’s natural beauty for future generations, ensuring our industry remains vibrant and responsible.”
Retail Sector Employee: “Adopting sustainable practices in our retail operations, from reducing waste to sourcing ethically, can help mitigate climate change impacts and appeal to environmentally conscious consumers.”

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Invisible Bandwagon

Researchers changed one line in a tax notice:
“9 out of 10 people in your area pay their taxes on time.”
Compliance jumped 15%.
Not moral appeal. Not threats. Just: you’re the weird one if you don’t.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Science

This is Social Proof + Normative Messaging.
But the key is: local and specific.

“Most people” → weak
“Most people in your building” → strong
“Most people in your building with kids your age” → irresistible

The more similar the reference group, the more powerful the pull.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
You Just Saw It

The best arguments today didn’t say “people care about climate.”
They said:
“Engineers at HKU are already shifting their research priorities.”
A bandwagon that didn’t exist until you named it.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

Create a bandwagon for your argument.
Make it local. Make it specific. Make them the outlier if they disagree.

title: The Persuasion Playbook
text:
Quick Callback

Last week’s strategy: Sensory Hijack
The body believes what the mind resists.
Anyone try it? Did you make your audience physically uncomfortable — on purpose?

title: The Persuasion Playbook
text:
The Anchoring Trap

Realtors show you a terrible, overpriced house first.
The second house — still overpriced — looks reasonable.
You don’t evaluate houses. You evaluate differences from the anchor.

title: The Persuasion Playbook
text:
The Science

This is Anchoring (Tversky & Kahneman, 1974).
The first number you hear distorts all subsequent judgment — even when it’s obviously irrelevant.
In experiments, people estimated higher values after spinning a random wheel with higher numbers.
The brain cannot ignore the anchor.

title: The Persuasion Playbook
text:
You Just Saw It

When someone opened with an extreme number:
“AI could consume 30% of global electricity by 2040. Even the conservative estimate is 12%.”
Now 12% sounds reasonable. But 12% is still catastrophic.
The anchor did its job.

title: The Persuasion Playbook
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

Lead with the extreme. Then offer the “reasonable” version.
Set the anchor before they do.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Quick Callback

Last week’s strategy: The Pre-Mortem
Assume you failed. Now fix it.
Anyone try it? Did you imagine your argument bombing and then strengthen the weak points?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: Something You Took Away Last Week
text:
Something You Took Away Last Week

Week 7 showed you the Doubt Machine — how doubt is manufactured as a product, not discovered as a fact.
The Hockey Stick Graph attacked. Climategate emails cherry-picked. Exxon’s own scientists silenced. Same playbook as tobacco — fund alternative research, amplify dissenters, delay action for decades.
Epistemic humility: when someone says “the science isn’t settled,” ask who profits from your uncertainty?


Now apply that lens to the biggest thing on Earth that most people never think about.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: Quick W7 Recap: The Doubt Machine
text:
Quick W7 Recap: The Doubt Machine

Three things from last week you need to hold onto:
1. “Doubt is our product” — Brown & Williamson Tobacco, 1969. The same PR firms went from denying cancer to denying climate change.
2. The Hockey Stick — a graph so dangerous to fossil fuel profits that a mining executive and an economist were recruited to attack its statistics. Dozens of independent teams have since confirmed it.
3. The pattern — you don’t need to win the debate. You just need to make people think there is one.


Now: what happens when the Doubt Machine makes an entire planet-scale system invisible?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
From Manufactured Doubt → Invisible Infrastructure

Last week: “What if the truth itself is contested?”
This week: “What if the most important system on Earth is one you can’t even see?”
The ocean.
It covers 71% of the planet. It absorbs 30% of our CO₂. It traps 90% of excess heat. And the way we study it — who gets to research it, who profits from it, whose knowledge counts — is shaped by the same power structures you studied last week.


Your toolkit: Spectacle Formula → Complexity → System Boundaries → Timing → Built Environment → Structural Incentives → The Doubt Machine → now: Invisible Infrastructure.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Coming Up: Week 9 Preview

Next week, we meet the people who are already drowning.
The Carteret Islands are sinking. Their residents produce virtually zero carbon. The nations that caused the crisis? They’re building sea walls — for themselves.
Responsibility asymmetry: the people who suffer most aren’t the ones who caused it.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
We’ve been building responses to climate change, one topic after another.
Before we dive into today — a quick recap of where we’ve been.
