title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
A turtle entangled in a ghost net — abandoned fishing gear drifting through the ocean. 46% of the Great Pacific Garbage Patch is fishing nets, not consumer plastic.




You were told to stop using plastic straws. This is what’s actually killing the ocean.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Hero Image

That slide just did something to you. Let’s name it.
A hero image is a single photograph that carries the entire argument.
No bullet points. No data tables. No charts. Just one frame — and your audience does the rest.
This is the Spectacle Formula from Week 1, distilled:
Fact (46% of ocean trash is fishing nets) + Story (a living animal trapped) + Stakes (this is happening right now) = an image you can’t unsee.


Today, watch how we use hero images. Then use one in your own presentation.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: Two Sides. Two Approaches to the Ocean.
text:
Two Sides. Two Approaches to the Ocean.



PRO-CLIMATE


= The Ocean Is a Climate Machine
= “Protect the carbon sink first”




PRO-DEVELOPMENT


= The Ocean Is a Resource
= “3 billion people depend on it for protein”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Core Tension








PRO-CLIMATE
PRO-DEVELOPMENT




Restrict industrial fishing to protect carbon sinks
Scale sustainable fishing to feed the world


Ban bottom trawling — it releases stored CO₂
Manage trawling — banning it kills livelihoods


Integrate indigenous knowledge
Prioritize peer-reviewed science


The ocean is a climate system
The ocean is a food system





This week: is the ocean a carbon sink to protect, or a resource to harvest?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: Quick Exercise: What Comes to Mind?
text:
Quick Exercise: What Comes to Mind?

When I say “ocean and climate change” —
what keywords come to mind?


Shout them out. Let’s collect them.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: What You Probably Said vs. What You Didn’t
text:
What You Probably Said vs. What You Didn’t



Common Answers



Plastic pollution
Sea level rise
Polar bears / ice caps
Coral bleaching
Overfishing
Pollution / oil spills





Rarely Mentioned



The carbon pump
Thermohaline circulation
Phytoplankton (50% of O₂)
Ocean acidification
Bottom trawling emissions
Ocean heat absorption (90%)





The gap between those two columns? That’s the invisible infrastructure.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Let’s talk about the single biggest blindspot of climate change.


The ocean.


It absorbs 90% of excess heat. It absorbs 30% of our CO₂. It produces 50% of our oxygen.
And it barely makes the news.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Why “Blindspot”?

Scientists know. The data is overwhelming. Thousands of papers, decades of research.
The blindspot isn’t in science. It’s in public discourse and policy.

Climate debates focus on land: cars, factories, buildings, deforestation
The Paris Agreement barely mentions the ocean
The plastic crisis gets attention — but for straws, not for the carbon pump
Ocean acidification — “the evil twin” — gets almost zero airtime

Why? Because no one profits from making the ocean visible. There’s no fossil fuel lobby for the deep sea. No election won on phytoplankton.


Week 7’s lesson: what gets attention is shaped by who profits from visibility.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
So What Does “Ocean, Decolonized” Mean?

The ocean has been colonized by three wrong narratives. Today we strip them away.





The Resource Narrative



We think of the ocean as fish, shipping lanes, oil.
Today: the ocean is a climate machine — carbon pump, thermostat, oxygen factory.





The Consumer Guilt Narrative



We think the problem is our plastic straw.
Today: the real damage is industrial trawling, acidification, and a broken carbon pump.





The Knowledge Narrative



We think Western science is the only valid lens.
Today: indigenous communities understood the ocean for millennia before satellites existed.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Blue Marble — Apollo 17, 1972. This is not a land planet with some water. This is an ocean planet with some land.




You live on an ocean planet. You just forgot.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Numbers

The ocean covers 71% of Earth’s surface.
It circulates 83% of global CO₂.
It absorbs 30% of our CO₂ emissions.
It traps 90% of excess greenhouse heat.


And yet — we’ve explored less than 20% of it. We know more about the surface of Mars.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Where Does the Heat Go?

Change in total heat content of Earth — the ocean absorbs over 93% of excess heat. Ice, land, and atmosphere share the rest.
The ocean isn’t just near the problem. It’s absorbing almost all of it.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Global Conveyor Belt

The ocean isn’t a bathtub. It’s a circulation engine.
Thermohaline circulation — driven by temperature (thermo) and salt (haline):

Warm surface water flows from the tropics toward the poles
At the poles, it cools, becomes denser, and sinks to the deep ocean
Deep water creeps back toward the equator over centuries
One full cycle takes roughly 1,000 years

If this conveyor slows down, Europe freezes. If it stops, global climate collapses.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Conveyor Belt, Visualised

Thermohaline circulation — red = warm surface currents, blue = cold deep currents. This system distributes heat, regulates weather, and drives nutrient cycles across the planet.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Biological Carbon Pump

The ocean sequesters carbon through a living machine:
1. Phytoplankton — microscopic algae at the ocean surface

They produce 50% of Earth’s oxygen — more than all forests combined
They absorb CO₂ through photosynthesis, just like trees
When they die, they sink — carrying carbon to the deep ocean floor

2. Fish and whales — the pump’s delivery system

Fish eat plankton, excrete carbon-rich waste that sinks
Whales feed deep, defecate at the surface — fertilising phytoplankton with iron and nitrogen

3. The seabed — the planet’s largest carbon vault

Sediments on the ocean floor store billions of tonnes of carbon, accumulated over millennia

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Carbon Pump, Visualised

The biological carbon pump — CO₂ absorbed at the surface, fixed by phytoplankton, sinks as particulate organic carbon, stored in deep sediments.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The ocean is not just affected by climate change.
The ocean is the climate system.
Break the ocean, break the climate.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Everything This Semester Connects Here









Week
Concept
How It Shows Up in the Ocean




W1
Spectacle Formula
The hero images today — one turtle, one argument


W2
Complexity
The ocean is a complex system — circulation, carbon pump, food webs — not reducible to “just pollution”


W3
System Boundaries
Where does plastic’s lifecycle end? Oil well → factory → ocean → phytoplankton → your bloodstream


W4
Timing
The conveyor belt takes 1,000 years per cycle. Our damage is measured in decades. Timing mismatch.


W5
Built Environment
40% of humanity lives within 100 km of the coast. The built environment meets the ocean.


W6
Structural Incentives
The ocean is the ultimate commons. $35B/year in subsidies to overexploit it. Tragedy of the Commons, at sea.


W7
The Doubt Machine
“The trawling science is debated” — but the physics of acidification is settled. Know the difference.


W8
Invisible Infrastructure
All of the above, happening beneath the surface where no one looks.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Two Layers of Damage

The ocean’s capacity to regulate climate is being degraded. But we need to be honest about what we know:




Layer 1: Settled Physics


IPCC-grade, measurable, undeniable:

Warmer water holds less CO₂ (Henry’s Law)
More CO₂ absorbed → more acidic → harder to absorb more
Warming creates stratification — surface layer won’t mix with deep water
The ocean’s efficiency as a carbon sink is measurably declining





Layer 2: Emerging Science


Directionally alarming, still being quantified:

Bottom trawling may release ~1 Gt CO₂/year (Sala et al., Nature 2021 — debated)
Microplastics appear to reduce phytoplankton growth (lab studies; ocean-scale impact uncertain)
Whale depletion reduces phytoplankton fertilisation (real mechanism; exact carbon impact still being estimated)

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Why the Two-Layer Distinction Matters

Layer 1 alone is already catastrophic. Even without trawling or plastic, the physics of warming + acidification is measurably reducing the ocean’s capacity.
Layer 2 may be compounding the damage — but we’re still quantifying how much.
This is epistemic humility from Week 7 in action:

We don’t need to wait for perfect Layer 2 data when Layer 1 is already a crisis
But we also shouldn’t overstate emerging science — that’s the Doubt Machine’s trick in reverse
The honest position: “The physics is certain. The biology is alarming. Both demand action.”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: How Ocean Currents Create Garbage Patches
text:
How Ocean Currents Create Garbage Patches

The ocean’s surface currents form 5 massive circular systems called gyres — driven by wind, the Earth’s rotation, and temperature differences.
Think of them as slow-motion whirlpools, each spanning thousands of kilometres:

North Pacific Gyre — between Asia and North America
South Pacific Gyre — the most remote water on Earth
North Atlantic Gyre — between Europe, Africa, and the Americas
South Atlantic Gyre — below the equator
Indian Ocean Gyre — between Africa, Asia, and Australia

Anything that enters a gyre gets trapped in the centre — pulled inward by converging currents, with no way out.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Five Gyres, Mapped

The 5 major oceanic gyres — circular current systems that trap everything from nutrients to plastic. The two largest garbage patches sit in the North Pacific and North Atlantic gyres.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Two Trash Patches. Same Mechanism.

The Great Pacific Garbage Patch (North Pacific Gyre)

Estimated at 1.6 million km² — 3x the size of France
~80,000 tonnes of plastic trapped and circulating
94% is microplastics — invisible to the naked eye
46% by mass is abandoned fishing nets

The North Atlantic Garbage Patch (North Atlantic Gyre)

Smaller but denser — concentrated between Bermuda and the Azores
Estimated at hundreds of kilometres across
Same composition: microplastics, fishing gear, industrial waste



These aren’t floating islands you can walk on. They’re plastic soups — clouds of particles suspended in the water column. You could sail through one and never see it.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: The Great Pacific Garbage Patch: Mapped
text:
The Great Pacific Garbage Patch: Mapped

Plastic mass concentration in the North Pacific — scientific model based on ocean surveys. The red core is 3x the size of France. (Lebreton et al., Nature Scientific Reports, 2018)

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Where the Patches Form

NOAA map of marine debris accumulation in the North Pacific Gyre — the Eastern and Western garbage patches form where converging currents trap everything that enters.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
46% of the Great Pacific Garbage Patch
is fishing nets.
Not your plastic straw.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
A blue plastic bag draped over a living fan coral — Wakatobi, Indonesia. Two crises in one frame.




The coral took decades to grow. The bag took seconds to drop.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Numbers


Over 400 million tonnes of plastic produced each year
Breaks down into microplastics (≤ 5mm) under UV light

Found in human blood, placental tissue, and breast milk
Biomagnification up the food chain

Degrades over hundreds to thousands of years



You are eating, drinking, and breathing plastic. Right now.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
How Plastic Drives Climate Change

Plastic isn’t just polluting the ocean. It connects to climate change at every stage:
Settled science:

Plastic is made from petroleum and natural gas — production accounts for ~3.4% of global greenhouse emissions
At current growth rates, plastic could consume 20% of all oil production by 2050
When plastic degrades under UV light, it releases methane and ethylene — potent greenhouse gases

Emerging research (directionally alarming):

Microplastics are ingested by phytoplankton — the organisms that produce 50% of Earth’s oxygen
Lab studies suggest microplastics can significantly reduce phytoplankton growth and photosynthesis
If this scales to the real ocean: less phytoplankton → less CO₂ absorbed → more warming
We don’t yet know the full ocean-scale impact — but the direction is clear

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The fossil fuel industry makes the plastic.
The plastic enters the ocean.
The ocean’s carbon pump weakens.
The climate gets worse.
The industry that caused climate change is also degrading the system that regulates it.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: How Fishing Connects to Climate Change
text:
How Fishing Connects to Climate Change

Settled science:

The ocean’s biological carbon pump depends on healthy marine food webs — fish and whales transport carbon from the surface to the deep ocean
Industrial whaling reduced whale populations by 66–90% — whales fertilise phytoplankton through iron-rich faeces at the surface. Fewer whales = less fertilisation = less CO₂ absorbed.

Emerging research (significant but debated):

Bottom trawling scrapes the seabed, disturbing carbon-rich sediments accumulated over millennia
Sala et al. (Nature, 2021) estimated this releases ~1 Gt CO₂/year — comparable to global aviation
This figure is debated — critics argue much of the disturbed carbon may be recaptured by the water column before reaching the atmosphere
But even conservative estimates put trawling emissions in the hundreds of millions of tonnes per year

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Bottom trawling releases an estimated 0.5–1 Gt CO₂/year.
Global aviation releases ~1 Gt CO₂/year.
One of them is regulated. One of them is subsidised.


Governments spend $35 billion per year subsidising fishing fleets. Many would be unprofitable without the subsidy.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Coral under heat stress turns vivid, fluorescent colours — a last-ditch survival response before full bleaching and death.




This is not beauty. This is a death signal.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Why Reefs Matter

Coral reefs protect coastlines from storms, provide jobs, and sustain fisheries.
Over 0.5 billion people depend on them for food, income, and protection. (NOAA)
Reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor — but support 25% of all marine species.
Losing them isn’t just an ecological disaster. It’s an economic collapse for entire coastlines.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Double Strike

Coral is being hit by two forces at once:
Warming → Bleaching

Coral expels the algae that give it colour and food when water temperatures rise
Without the algae, coral starves. If temperatures don’t drop within weeks, it dies.

Acidification → Dissolution

Acidic seawater dissolves calcium carbonate — the material coral skeletons are made of
Coral can’t build its skeleton as fast as the water dissolves it

Warming bleaches them. Acidification dissolves them. Together, it’s a death sentence.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
50% of the world’s coral reefs
have died since 1950.
At current rates, 90% will be gone by 2050.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: See It: How Dead Is the Great Barrier Reef?
text:
See It: How Dead Is the Great Barrier Reef?


Vox (~6 min). “Have we really killed the Great Barrier Reef? No. But we sure are trying.” Coral bleaching explained with the discomfort intact.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Healthy corals alongside bleaching corals on the same reef — the before and after, side by side.




Same reef. Same water. One side is alive. One side is dying. The difference is 2°C.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Everyone talks about the ocean warming.
Almost no one talks about the ocean dissolving.


Ocean acidification is climate change’s evil twin — and it’s already here.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Chemistry, Simply

When CO₂ dissolves in seawater, it forms carbonic acid.
The ocean’s pH has dropped by 0.1 units since the Industrial Revolution.
That sounds tiny. It’s not.

pH is a logarithmic scale — 0.1 = a 26% increase in acidity
Acidic water dissolves calcium carbonate — the material shells and coral skeletons are made of
Shellfish, coral, plankton — the base of the marine food web — are literally dissolving



The ocean absorbed our CO₂ for centuries. Now it’s paying the price.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: The Evidence: CO₂ Up, pH Down
text:
The Evidence: CO₂ Up, pH Down

Hawaii Carbon Dioxide Time-Series — atmospheric CO₂ (Mauna Loa) rising alongside ocean CO₂ (Aloha station), while ocean pH steadily declines. The “double whammy” graph.
As atmospheric CO₂ rises, the ocean absorbs more — and its pH drops in lockstep.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: See It: The Evil Twin of Climate Change
text:
See It: The Evil Twin of Climate Change


Triona McGrath, TEDx (~9 min). An oceanographer explains how CO₂ is changing the ocean’s chemistry — and why it matters for everything that lives in it.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Decolonization Question

The research gap:

Ocean science is dominated by G7 countries — the same nations that industrialised first
Resources and data are extracted from less-resourced waters — often without fair compensation
Western scientific approaches are treated as the only valid lens

Indigenous knowledge — systematically dismissed:

Indigenous peoples have understood marine environments for millennia — tidal patterns, breeding cycles, migration routes, reef health
In the Arctic, Inuit communities detected sea ice changes years before satellite monitoring confirmed them
In the Pacific Islands, community-managed marine areas consistently outperform government-managed ones
This knowledge is disappearing as fishing communities shrink

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Fishing boats in Aberdeen Harbour, Hong Kong — where centuries of marine knowledge live alongside one of the world’s most developed coastlines.




This is Hong Kong’s relationship with the sea. It’s older than the skyline.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Overfishing in Hong Kong

The decline: Hong Kong’s once-rich waters have been dramatically depleted by decades of overfishing.
The ban: In 2012, Hong Kong banned trawling in its waters — one of the few places in the world to do so. Fish stocks have shown signs of recovery.
The knowledge gap: Local fishermen possess generational knowledge of sustainable practices, seasonal cycles, and early signs of ecosystem stress — knowledge that is disappearing as fishing communities shrink.
The climate connection: Hong Kong’s marine ecosystem is part of the South China Sea carbon cycle. Healthy fisheries = healthy carbon pump. Depleted fisheries = broken pump.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: HK Trawling Ban: A Case Study
text:
HK Trawling Ban: A Case Study

In 2012, Hong Kong became one of the first places in the world to ban bottom trawling in its waters.
PRO-CLIMATE: Fish stocks have recovered. Biodiversity is returning. The seabed — Hong Kong’s underwater carbon store — is no longer being scraped open.
PRO-DEVELOPMENT: Thousands of fishermen lost their livelihoods. Government compensation was inadequate. Many switched to illegal fishing or left the industry entirely.


The right policy, with the wrong transition plan. Sound familiar? (Week 6: Tragedy of the Commons.)

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Why This Motion?

This debate connects three scales — and everything we covered today:




Local (Hong Kong)


HK banned trawling in 2012. Fish stocks recovered. Fishermen lost livelihoods. The transition was messy. Can this model scale?




Global (Industry)


Industrial fishing fleets — subsidised by $35B/year — drag nets across the seabed and dump gear into the gyres. 46% of both garbage patches is fishing nets. The industry that feeds 3 billion people is also breaking the ocean.




Physics (The Machine)


Trawling releases stored seabed carbon. Plastic degrades into greenhouse gases. Both disrupt phytoplankton — the base of the carbon pump. The settled physics says the ocean is losing capacity. The emerging science says we’re accelerating it.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
This Week’s Debate Motion

“This house believes that industrial fishing is a climate crime and should be regulated under the Paris Agreement.”


PRO-CLIMATE: Bottom trawling releases as much CO₂ as aviation. Fishing nets are 46% of ocean plastic. This isn’t food production — it’s climate destruction at industrial scale.
PRO-DEVELOPMENT: 3 billion people depend on fish for protein. Calling fishing a “climate crime” threatens food security for the world’s poorest.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text: Let’s Assign Groups

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Debate

You will assume stakeholder positions: Local Fishermen, Marine Scientists, Policy Makers, Fishing Industry Representatives, Conservation NGOs, Consumer Advocates — or whatever you see fit.
Remember the three scales:

Local — what does this mean for Hong Kong’s fishing communities?
Global — what does this mean for the $35B fishing subsidy system?
Physics — what does this mean for the carbon pump?

Build arguments that connect all three. The strongest debaters today will be the ones who move between scales.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Ground Rules


This is a safe space for exploration and discussion. There are no wrong answers, only opportunities to learn.
Respect and open-mindedness are our guiding principles. Every opinion shared contributes to collective learning.
Feedback and reflection are encouraged. This is a chance to voice thoughts, ask questions, and grow from the experience.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Pro-Climate Talking Points

The Ocean Is a Climate System — Treat It Like One:

Bottom trawling releases an estimated 0.5–1 Gt CO₂/year — comparable to global aviation, yet it receives $35B in government subsidies
Industrial fishing nets make up 46% of ocean plastic by mass — the biggest contributor to the garbage patches
Overfishing disrupts the biological carbon pump — fewer fish = less carbon reaching the deep ocean
Hong Kong’s trawling ban (2012) shows it can be done — fish stocks have recovered
Indigenous fishermen’s knowledge can guide sustainable alternatives that protect both livelihoods and carbon sinks

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Pro-Development Talking Points

Food Security Cannot Be Sacrificed:

3 billion people depend on fish as their primary source of protein — regulation must be gradual
The trawling CO₂ figure is debated — critics argue much disturbed carbon is recaptured before reaching the atmosphere
Classifying fishing as a “climate crime” will devastate coastal economies in developing nations
Technology solutions (selective gear, satellite monitoring, AI stock modelling) can reduce impact without banning industries
Hong Kong’s trawling ban displaced thousands of workers — the transition was poorly managed
The Paris Agreement should focus on the biggest emitters (energy, transport), not food production

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
What Just Happened?

Notice what happened across today’s slides.
Every section opened with one image, one caption. No bullet points. No data. Just a frame that forces your brain to fill in the story.
Why it works:

The brain processes images 60,000x faster than text
Emotion precedes logic — the image lands before the argument
A hero image doesn’t explain the problem. It makes the audience feel it first, then seek the explanation.



Your challenge: find ONE image for your portfolio that does this. The rest of the slide deck is just commentary.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: What You Can’t See Still Matters Most
text:
What You Can’t See Still Matters Most

Before this week, “climate change” might have felt like a land problem — cities, farms, factories.
Now you know: the ocean is the planet’s climate machine.

The physics is certain: warming and acidification are measurably reducing the ocean’s capacity as a carbon sink
The biology is alarming: overfishing, plastic, and trawling appear to be compounding the damage
The honest position: we don’t need perfect data on every mechanism when the settled science alone is already a crisis



Your portable takeaway: The ocean isn’t a victim of climate change. It is the climate system. Break the ocean, break the climate.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Did We Decolonize?

At the start of today, we named three narratives that colonized the ocean:
1. The resource narrative — “the ocean is fish and shipping lanes” → Now you know it’s a climate machine: carbon pump, thermostat, oxygen factory.
2. The consumer guilt narrative — “stop using plastic straws” → Now you know it’s industrial: trawling emissions rival aviation, fishing nets are 46% of the garbage patch, plastic is a fossil fuel product.
3. The knowledge narrative — “only peer-reviewed Western science counts” → Now you know indigenous communities understood the ocean before satellites existed — and their marine areas often outperform government-managed ones.


Decolonizing doesn’t mean rejecting science. It means asking: whose story shaped what you saw — and what you missed?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Fork

“Are you going to let your family suffer, or are you going to act now?”
The trick isn’t the answer.
The trick is that both options accept your frame.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Science

This is the False Dilemma — or in sales, The Fork.
Present two choices that both serve your goal.
“Do you want the red one or the blue one?” (Not: “Do you want to buy a car?”)
The brain loves binary choices. Multiple options = paralysis.
Two options = decision.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
You Just Saw It

When someone said:
“We can either regulate AI now, or let corporations decide for us.”
Notice what’s missing?
“Do nothing” wasn’t an option. Because they didn’t put it there.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

End with two options — both of which support your case.
Remove the third option before they think of it.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Quick Callback

Last week’s strategy: The Anchoring Trap
First number wins.
Anyone try it? Did you lead with an extreme number before offering your “reasonable” version?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: Something You Took Away Last Week
text:
Something You Took Away Last Week

Week 5 showed you that every building is a climate decision.
The Urban Heat Island effect — concrete absorbs heat, cities get hotter, AC demand rises, more fossil fuels burn, cities get even hotter.
You saw it in NYC (one park = 5°C difference), Vienna (turning trash into heat for 60,000 homes), and LA (paying people to rip out their lawns).


The built environment is a climate actor. But here’s the question: even if we know the right design, who decides what gets built?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: From Buildings → The Rules That Build Them
text:
From Buildings → The Rules That Build Them

Last week: “What did we build, and what does it cost the planet?”
This week: “Why don’t we build better — and whose fault is that?”
Same system. Bigger question.
You can design the greenest building in the world, but if the economic incentives reward cheap construction and short-term profit, it won’t get built. The problem isn’t engineering — it’s structural incentives.


Your toolkit: Spectacle Formula → Complexity → System Boundaries → Timing → Built Environment → now: Structural Incentives.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Rondônia, Brazil — NASA satellite, 2006. Each fishbone is a road. Each branch is a cleared farm. This used to be unbroken rainforest.




No one decided to destroy the Amazon. Everyone just followed the incentives.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100

Two Sides. Two Economic Worldviews.



PRO-CLIMATE

PRO-CLIMATE

System change needed

Green growth

This tension appears in every economic climate debate.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Oldest Energy Source

When was the earliest of all times that humans started to harvest energy?


Campfire.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text: Dark clouds of factory smoke obscure Clark Avenue Bridge, Cleveland, Ohio — US National Archives, 1970.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
From Campfire to Capital

Energy at scale created surplus. Surplus created exchange. Exchange created markets. Markets created capital.

1st Industrial Revolution → coal, steam, factory
2nd Industrial Revolution → oil, electricity, mass production
Energy surplus at scale → source, transportation, end-user, importer

The chain: energy → surplus → market → currency → capital → and now, war.


Energy issues don’t arise in isolation — they arise at scale. And at scale, they become geopolitics.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
“It is not from the benevolence of the butcher, the brewer, or the baker that we expect our dinner, but from their regard to their own interest.”


— Adam Smith, The Wealth of Nations, Book I, Chapter 2, 1776


Self-interest as engine. That’s the promise of capitalism — and the root of its climate problem.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
What Is Capital?

The accumulated wealth of an individual, company, or community, used as a fund for carrying on fresh production.
Wealth in any form used to help produce more wealth.


And what is capitalism?


“Capitalism” and “Capitalist” are 19th-century pejoratives — names given by its enemies. But the system itself has two faces:

Enormous productive capacity
Always on the edge of being out of control

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Who Is This Man?





Photo by Edward Steichen.





Does he look warm and fuzzy?
What’s he holding?

This is J.P. Morgan — the man who personally bailed out the US government. Twice.
Capitalism has always had a face. Usually holding a cigar.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Capitalism Is… (Per Karl Marx)


A disease for which scientific socialism is the cure
A method to steal the labour of the exploited masses
A system that undermines every traditionally established way of making a living
A system that inevitably undermines its own foundations.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Capitalism Is… (Per Current Definitions)

A system defined by:

Private deployment of capital
Open acknowledgement of profit as a motive
Sympathetic understanding of self/household interests
Tolerance for innovation — though the characteristic fact of historical society is resistance to innovation as a kind of human nature



Hundreds of variations exist, but the essentials remain unchanged.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Tragedy of the Commons

When individuals prioritise their own interests over common goods, the commons collapses.
This applies to:

Individuals — driving, flying, consuming
Corporations — externalising pollution costs
Nations — free-riding on others’ emission cuts



A new economic system may help. But how?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
“Ruin is the destination toward which all men rush, each pursuing his own best interest in a society that believes in the freedom of the commons.”


— Garrett Hardin, “The Tragedy of the Commons”, Science, Vol. 162, 1968

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: See It: The Pattern That Keeps Repeating
text:
See It: The Pattern That Keeps Repeating


TED-Ed (~5 min). The Tragedy of the Commons — from medieval grazing to climate collapse. The twist: communal management often works better than privatisation.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
One Image. One Question.

BP invented the concept of a “personal carbon footprint” in 2005.
It was an ad campaign.
You were the product.


Structural problems require structural solutions. Individual guilt is a distraction.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
“It is difficult to get a man to understand something, when his salary depends upon his not understanding it.”


— Upton Sinclair, I, Candidate for Governor: And How I Got Licked, 1935, p. 109


This is why oil executives don’t “believe in” climate change. They understand it fine. They’re paid not to.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Carbon Pricing: Pay to Pollute?

The usual market solution to climate change: price the pollution.








Mechanism
How It Works
The Problem




Carbon Tax
Fixed price per tonne of CO₂
Who sets the price?


Carbon Credit
Right to emit, tradeable
Government selling right to pollute?


Carbon Offset
Pay someone else to reduce
Does it actually reduce anything?





What happens when the price target moves? When carbon becomes a commodity to trade for profit?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Now imagine you’re a government.
Oil just jumped 9% overnight because of a military strike 8,000 km away.
Your citizens are paying more for petrol, electricity, groceries — everything.


And you want to add a carbon tax on top of that?


That’s not policy. That’s political suicide. And every fossil fuel lobby knows it.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Trap

This is the structural problem:

Peacetime: carbon tax is unpopular but survivable
Crisis: carbon tax becomes impossible — voters revolt
Oil companies know this. They don’t need to fight the tax. They just need to wait for the next crisis.

And there is always a next crisis.


The PRO-CLIMATE position has a timing problem. The PRO-DEVELOPMENT position has a moral problem. Neither side wants to admit it.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text: Paro Taktsang (Tiger’s Nest), Bhutan — a country that measures Gross National Happiness instead of GDP.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Post-Growth Challenge

A continued growth mindset is unsustainable. But what replaces it?




Alternative Models



Bhutan — Gross National Happiness Index: well-being, conservation, cultural preservation
New Zealand — Wellbeing Budget: mental health, child poverty, environment over GDP
Nordic Model — free market + comprehensive welfare + collective bargaining





Collective Ownership



Mondragon (Spain) — largest worker cooperative; workers own and operate
Community Land Trusts (Boston, London) — nonprofit collective land ownership for affordable housing
Germany — social market economy: free enterprise + social welfare + environmental protection

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text: Oil palm plantation, Cigudeg, Indonesia — monoculture replacing rainforest. The market’s answer to “efficiency.”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Mixed Economy: The Singapore Question

Singapore’s blend: competitive open markets + strong state intervention in housing, healthcare, and public transport.
Germany’s blend: capitalist economy + framework of social policies balancing enterprise with welfare and environmental protection.


The question isn’t capitalism vs. socialism. It’s: what mix — and who decides?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
On 28 February 2026, the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran.
Within 72 hours:

Oil jumped ~9%
The Strait of Hormuz effectively closed
15 million barrels/day stopped moving
Qatar’s LNG facilities were hit by Iranian drones



This isn’t a history slide. This is happening right now.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Every Player Has an Agenda









Player
What “Winning” Looks Like
The Energy Angle




USA
Regime change. Destroy nuclear programme. Declare victory in <30 days.
Controls narrative but not the oil price. Domestic gas prices already spiking.


Israel
Iran implodes. Regional threat eliminated permanently.
No direct energy stake — but Gulf allies are furious.


Iran
Survive. Drag it out. Make the cost unbearable.
Strait of Hormuz is the weapon. Close it = global recession.


Saudi Arabia / Gulf States
Don’t get hit. Stay neutral. Protect oil infrastructure.
Iranian drones already struck airports, hotels, oil installations. Their worst nightmare.


China
Stability. Buys 90% of Iran’s oil exports.
40% of China’s oil imports transit Hormuz. Acute supply risk.


Europe
Was told “minutes” before strikes began. Struggling to be heard.
LNG from Qatar = 20% of global supply, now disrupted.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Chain Effect

Step 1: Strait of Hormuz closes → 31% of global seaborne crude halts
Step 2: Oil supertanker rates hit all-time high ($424,000/day, up 94%)
Step 3: Insurers cancel war-risk cover in the Gulf → shipping freezes
Step 4: Qatar LNG facilities hit → 20% of global LNG supply offline
Step 5: Asia absorbs the pain — 70% of Hormuz crude goes to China, India, Japan, South Korea
Step 6: Thailand tells citizens not to panic-buy fuel. Pakistan and Bangladesh face blackouts.


One chokepoint. One week. The entire global energy system shudders.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Where is Hong Kong in this chain?


90% of HK’s electricity comes from fossil fuels.
How much of that fuel transits the Strait of Hormuz?


The built environment from Week 5 doesn’t just trap heat. It runs on a supply chain that passes through a war zone.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: Why This Belongs in a Capitalism Lecture
text:
Why This Belongs in a Capitalism Lecture

Every concept from today is in this story:







This Week’s Concept
Where It Shows Up




Structural Incentives
Every player acts rationally — the outcome is catastrophic


Tragedy of the Commons
The Strait of Hormuz is the ultimate commons


Carbon Pricing
Your carbon tax is meaningless when oil jumps 9% overnight


Disaster Capitalism
Oil companies and arms manufacturers profit from the crisis


Energy → Capital → War
The chain we traced from campfire ends here





The market doesn’t solve this. The market is this.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Formula (Reminder)

Fact + Human Story + Stakes = Spectacle




Weak

“Capitalism causes emissions”



Better

“100 companies produce 71% of global emissions”

“While you recycle, Shell knew about climate change in 1988 and spent millions denying it”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
PRO-CLIMATE: Make It Personal

Don’t say: “Carbon pricing has limitations.”
Say: “They want you to pay more for petrol while ExxonMobil gets tax breaks. You’re being charged for their mess.”
Don’t say: “We need systemic change.”
Say: “Your grandfather could afford a house on one salary. You can’t afford rent on two. That’s not laziness — that’s a system extracting everything from you.”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
PRO-DEVELOPMENT: Paint the Picture

Don’t say: “Markets drive innovation.”
Say: “In 2010, solar cost $378/MWh. Today: $36. That’s not government mandates — that’s competition. Capitalism did that.”
Don’t say: “We need economic growth.”
Say: “My grandmother grew up without electricity in rural China. Capitalism gave her grandchildren air conditioning, smartphones, and choices. Don’t take that away in the name of the planet.”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: What If the New Model Is Wrong?
text:
What If the New Model Is Wrong?

Think Tesla. (If you have to.)
What happens if we switch to a new economic model and it fails?







Risk
What Goes Wrong




Economic Slowdown
Growth stalls, jobs disappear


Investment Displacement
Capital moves to the wrong sectors


Innovation Misdirection
We solve the wrong problem (e.g. biofuels → deforestation)


Cost of Living
Carbon tax reflected in daily expenses


Energy Insecurity
One military strike closes the Strait of Hormuz — 15M barrels/day vanish


Inequality
Transition costs fall on those who can’t afford them





The risk of changing is real. But so is the risk of not changing.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
This Week’s Debate Motion

“This house believes that Hong Kong should impose a mandatory carbon tax on all businesses to fund its 2050 carbon neutrality target.”


PRO-CLIMATE: Make polluters pay — structural change requires structural funding.
PRO-DEVELOPMENT: A carbon tax will kill HK’s competitiveness — innovation, not taxation, drives change.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100

Create Your Persona



Lean PRO-CLIMATE? Try being a…

Labour union organiser fighting for just transition
Economist advocating degrowth

Youth climate striker






Lean PRO-DEVELOPMENT? Try being a…

Ask yourself: What do I stand to lose? That’s where your story begins.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: Human Story: The Shenzhen Factory Worker
text:
Human Story: The Shenzhen Factory Worker

Mei Li worked 12-hour shifts making electronics for $400/month. Her factory was shut down for “environmental violations.”
PRO-CLIMATE says: “Finally! That factory was poisoning the river. Workers like Mei deserve clean air.”
PRO-DEVELOPMENT says: “Mei lost her job. Her family went hungry. Now she begs on the street. Was the clean river worth it?”
The real question: How do we transition without leaving Mei behind?


Both narratives are emotionally powerful. Both are incomplete. Your job: Find the fuller story.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Specificity Illusion

Which is more believable?
A: “The project will take a few weeks.”
B: “The project will take 17 days.”
B. Always B. Even if both are equally made up.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Science

This is the Specificity Heuristic.
The brain uses detail as a proxy for credibility. Specific = precise = probably measured = probably true.
Con artists and skilled liars know this: add irrelevant detail and people stop questioning.
“I was at the corner of 5th and Main at 3:47pm” beats “I was downtown that afternoon.”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
You Just Saw It

Arguments that landed today had unnecessary precision:

Not “lots of energy” → “415 TWh”
Not “many people” → “240 million people”
Not “recently” → “March 2024”

The detail signals: I didn’t make this up.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

Replace every vague word with a specific number, date, or name.
Even if no one checks — they’ll believe you more.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Cheatsheet: PRO-CLIMATE

Arguments for a Resilient HK Economy:

Green tech startups — foster a competitive market for renewable energy
Circular economy models — test sustainable business practices in HK
Public-private partnerships — leverage private capital for smart city initiatives
Progressive regulation — environmental rules that encourage (not punish) innovation
Carbon pricing — incentivise low-carbon operations
Community-led initiatives — public awareness + local sustainability projects

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100

Cheatsheet: PRO-DEVELOPMENT

Arguments for HK’s Economic Stability:

Growth first — maintain a business-friendly environment, ensure job security
Proven strategies only — avoid untested models that risk market disruption
Minimal regulation — voluntary corporate sustainability, not mandates
Cost-benefit analysis — evaluate green initiatives on ROI, protect SMEs
Incremental improvements — gradual energy efficiency gains, low-risk green projects

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Fact-Check Guide



OK to Say



“Shell knew about climate change in 1988” (documented)
“Solar costs dropped 89% since 2010” (IEA data)
“Bhutan measures Gross National Happiness” (policy fact)





NOT OK



“Capitalism has killed millions” (vague, unverifiable)
“Green policies destroy all jobs” (exaggeration)
“Degrowth will solve everything” (unfounded claim)

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
Quick Callback

Last week’s strategy: The Barnum Slide
Statements that feel specific but apply to everyone create false intimacy.
Anyone try it? Did you write a sentence that felt personal but was actually universal?
Did faces react?

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
section: Something You Took Away Last Week
text:
Something You Took Away Last Week

Week 3 gave you a tool: Life Cycle Assessment.
You learned that where you draw the system boundary changes the entire answer.

Cotton tote vs. plastic bag? Depends on how many times you reuse it.
Beyond Burger vs. chicken? Depends on whether you count land use.
Organic cotton vs. conventional? Depends on which impact you measure.



That’s not just an LCA skill — that’s systems thinking. And you’ll need it again today.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
From Products → Energy Systems

Last week: “Is this product really green?”
This week: “Is this energy source really clean?”
Same question. Bigger system boundary.
Solar panels look perfect — until you widen the boundary to include timing (when the sun shines vs. when you need power), storage (where does the excess go?), and grid stability (who fills the gap at sunset?).


This is your portable toolkit — system-boundary thinking works for fashion, energy, food, transport. Every week builds on it.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
The Duck Curve — California ISO, 2016. The shape that changed the energy debate.




This one graph explains why solar energy alone can’t save us.

PRO-CLIMATE

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
Quick Poll

Which energy source has killed the most people per unit of electricity produced?

Nuclear
Coal
Solar
Wind

Vote now.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
section: The Answer (You Probably Got It Right)
text:
The Answer (You Probably Got It Right)

Coal kills approximately 24.6 people per TWh of electricity produced.
That includes mining accidents, air pollution, respiratory disease.
Nuclear? 0.03 deaths per TWh. Even including Chernobyl and Fukushima.


OK, that one was easy. But here’s where it gets weird…


Source: Markandya & Wilkinson (2007) The Lancet; Our World in Data (2023)

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
section: Wait — Solar and Wind Kill People?
text:
Wait — Solar and Wind Kill People?

Solar: 0.05 deaths per TWh. Wind: 0.04 deaths per TWh.
Both are higher than nuclear.
Rooftop installation falls. Manufacturing accidents. Mining for rare earth minerals.


The point: The energy source the public fears most (nuclear) is statistically the safest. The one they trust most (solar) is slightly more dangerous per kWh. Perception ≠ reality.


Source: Markandya & Wilkinson (2007); Sovacool et al. (2016) Journal of Cleaner Production

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text: The Duck Curve: midday solar floods the grid, evening demand spikes when the sun disappears. The gap is the problem.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
section: The Duck Curve: Three Problems in One
text:
The Duck Curve: Three Problems in One




The Belly (Midday)


Solar floods the grid when demand is moderate. Supply > demand. No battery storage at scale. Wholesale prices go negative — California pays Arizona to absorb the excess because it’s physically easier than shutting off thousands of rooftop panels.




The Neck (4–7 PM Ramp)


Sun sets. Solar drops to zero in ~3 hours. But demand spikes — everyone comes home, cooks, runs AC. The grid must add ~13,000 MW in 3 hours. Only gas peaker plants can ramp that fast. They’re expensive and dirty.




The Tail (Evening Peak)


All that solar capacity contributes nothing at the moment demand is highest. The evening peak is met almost entirely by fossil fuels — just like before solar existed.





The cruel irony: the more solar you build, the deeper the belly and the steeper the ramp. More solar makes the duck worse — until storage catches up.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
section: California: The Paradox on Maria’s Roof
text:
California: The Paradox on Maria’s Roof

Maria in San Diego installed rooftop solar in 2015. Her electricity bill dropped from $200/month to $12. She told everyone: “Solar pays for itself.”
Then San Diego Gas & Electric changed the rules. Time-of-use pricing meant Maria was selling power at midday — when it was worth almost nothing — and buying it back at peak evening rates.
Her savings dropped to $40/month. Her panels still worked perfectly. The economics didn’t.
Meanwhile, California was paying Arizona to take its excess solar power — negative wholesale prices. The grid couldn’t handle what it asked for.


Governor Schwarzenegger said “solar panels on every rooftop.” Nobody mentioned what happens when every rooftop turns on at the same time.


Sources: CAISO Duck Curve data; CPUC NEM 3.0 proceedings; San Diego Union-Tribune reporting

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
section: Germany: Klaus and the Dead Town
text:
Germany: Klaus and the Dead Town

Klaus worked in the lignite mines of Schwarze Pumpe, Lausitz, for 30 years. His father mined there. His grandfather too. Three generations of brown coal.
The Energiewende shut his mine. The government promised retraining. Klaus was 55.
Five years later: 60% of retrained workers in the Lausitz region were unemployed or in worse-paying jobs. The town’s population halved. The local school closed. The pub closed.
And Germany’s CO₂ emissions? Barely changed for years — because Berlin simultaneously shut down zero-carbon nuclear plants and temporarily burned more coal to fill the gap.


Klaus lost his livelihood for a transition that, for a decade, didn’t even reduce emissions.


Sources: Agora Energiewende; IW Köln regional labor studies; Clean Energy Wire; UBA emissions data

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
section: Ontario: The Bill That Brought Down a Government
text:
Ontario: The Bill That Brought Down a Government

The Henderson family in rural Ontario had a $120/month electricity bill in 2009. That year, Premier Dalton McGuinty signed the Green Energy Act. “We will create a new industry and thousands of jobs.”
Feed-in tariffs guaranteed solar producers 80 cents per kWh — when market price was 3–5 cents. Someone had to pay the difference. The Hendersons did.
By 2016, their bill was $250/month. The “Global Adjustment” charge — the hidden cost of renewable subsidies — was larger than the cost of actual electricity.
Ontario lost 300,000 manufacturing jobs (energy costs were one factor). In 2018, the governing Liberals won one seat. The worst electoral defeat in the province’s history.


Sources: Ontario Energy Board rate data; Ontario Auditor General (2015); Statistics Canada

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
section: What Do These Stories Have in Common?
text:
What Do These Stories Have in Common?

None of them are anti-renewable.
All of them are anti-simplicity.

California proves: timing and storage matter as much as generation.
Germany proves: sequencing matters — don’t kill nuclear before renewables can replace it.
Ontario proves: cost distribution matters — who pays for the transition?



The transition is necessary. The question is how to not destroy people along the way.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text: Castle Peak Power Station, Tuen Mun — one of the largest coal-fired plants in Asia.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
“Why Tuen Mun Again?”

In 2007, when the government proposed building another polluting facility in Tuen Mun, District Councillor Lung Shui-hing stood up:

“Why Tuen Mun again? The government is treating the district like a garbage dump where all the unwanted facilities are found.”

All 13 councillors voted unanimously against. They noted Tuen Mun already housed: Castle Peak Power Station, a recycling park, a steel mill, oil tanks, and a landfill.
Government officials later acknowledged that easterly winds — which carry pollutants away from the main urban areas — influenced site selection.


The pollution isn’t accidental. The location was chosen because the people living downwind were deemed less likely to complain.


Source: SCMP, August 18, 2007

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
Mrs Ho Can’t Stop Coughing

Mrs Ho, 56, developed an airway allergy she attributes partly to Hong Kong’s air pollution. She described it to SCMP:

“I coughed uncontrollably, drawing odd stares and dirty looks from people around me on the street.”

Dr Loretta So Kit-ying, respiratory medicine specialist: “Nitrogen dioxide will cause irritation to the airways, leading to a range of respiratory diseases.”
In 2015, Tuen Mun recorded 395 hours of “high” or above air quality readings — the worst in Hong Kong for the second consecutive year.


Sources: SCMP, Victor Ting, July 19, 2019; SCMP, Ernest Kao, November 30, 2015

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
section: The Price Tag on Hong Kong’s Air
text:
The Price Tag on Hong Kong’s Air



3,508

HK$42B


economic loss from pollution-related health costs annually




550%


how much HK’s roadside NO₂ exceeds WHO guidelines




Prof Anthony Hedley (HKU School of Public Health): “Probably 100 per cent of the population is exposed, at unacceptable levels, to this environmental hazard.”


Sources: Clean Air Network / HKU (2024 Review); Hedley Environmental Index (hedleyindex.hku.hk)

HK’s renewable potential could cover nearly half its electricity. The target is 7.5–10%. CLP says the future is nuclear imports. The city chose to outsource its energy conscience.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
The Motion

“Hong Kong should double its nuclear energy imports from mainland China to achieve carbon neutrality, rather than investing in local renewable energy.”


PRO-CLIMATE argues against. PRO-DEVELOPMENT argues for.
(Or does it? Which side are you really on?)

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
Why This Motion Is Hard



Against (PRO-CLIMATE?)



Energy dependence on mainland China
Nuclear waste and accident risk
HK has wind + solar potential (48%)
Local jobs in renewable sector
But… nuclear is zero-carbon. Opposing it means more fossil fuels now.





For (PRO-DEVELOPMENT?)



Nuclear is safest per kWh (0.03 deaths/TWh)
Reliable baseload — no Duck Curve problem
HK has no space for large-scale renewables
Proven, affordable, already 25% of supply
But… you’re outsourcing your energy future to another jurisdiction.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
The Formula (Reminder)

Fact + Human Story + Stakes = Spectacle




Weak

“Solar energy costs have decreased”



Better

“Solar costs dropped 89% since 2010”



Spectacle

“In 2010, solar was for hippies. In 2025, it’s cheaper than coal. The oil companies knew — and lied.”

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
PRO-CLIMATE: Make It Personal

Don’t say: “Fossil fuels cause emissions.”
Say: “Mrs Ho coughs uncontrollably on the street and strangers give her dirty looks. She lives downwind from Castle Peak. You’re paying for cheap electricity with her lungs.”
Don’t say: “We need renewable energy mandates.”
Say: “Germany did it. California did it. Hong Kong says ‘too expensive.’ Is your child’s health too expensive?”

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
PRO-DEVELOPMENT: Paint the Picture

Don’t say: “Energy transitions are complex.”
Say: “Ontario rushed renewables. Electricity bills doubled. Factories closed. Workers lost jobs. The government was wiped out. One seat. Is that the transition you want?”
Don’t say: “We need reliable baseload power.”
Say: “Germany spent €500 billion on renewables. When the wind doesn’t blow, they import nuclear from France. Klaus lost his job, his pension, his town. The carbon needle barely moved.”

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
Still Unsure About Your Persona?

If you’re still deciding on some traits, here are some starting points — mix, match, or invent your own:




Lean PRO-CLIMATE? Try being a…

Parent living downwind from Castle Peak
Youth activist who just read the IPCC report
Renewable energy entrepreneur
Doctor treating pollution-related illness






Lean PRO-DEVELOPMENT? Try being a…

Grid engineer keeping the lights on
Factory owner watching electricity bills climb
Government official balancing budgets
Coal worker whose town depends on the plant





Ask yourself: What do I stand to lose? That’s where your story begins.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
Remember: Fact-Check Your Stories



OK to Say



“Solar costs dropped 89% since 2010” (IEA data)

“Nuclear kills 0.03 per TWh” (Markandya & Wilkinson)

NOT OK



“Renewables can power everything now” (ignores intermittency)
“Green energy destroys economies” (exaggeration)
“Nuclear is perfectly safe” (overstated — low risk ≠ no risk)
“Oil companies are literally killing people” (unverifiable as stated)

Sensory Hijack

People holding a warm cup of coffee rated strangers as more trustworthy than those holding iced drinks.
They had no idea the cup mattered.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
The Science

This is Embodied Cognition (Williams & Bargh, 2008).
The body doesn’t just receive information — it shapes judgment.

Physical warmth → social warmth
Heaviness → seriousness
Roughness → difficulty

The brain takes sensory shortcuts constantly.

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
section: You Just Saw It — In the Energy Debate
text:
You Just Saw It — In the Energy Debate

The arguments that landed today weren’t abstract policy statements.
They made you feel something in your body.
Not “coal combustion produces particulate matter affecting respiratory health.”
But: “Mrs Ho coughs uncontrollably on the street. Strangers give her dirty looks. She doesn’t know how to explain that the air itself is making her sick.”
Not “energy transitions have economic costs.”
But: “Klaus is 55. His mine closed. His pension vanished. His town is dying.”


That’s sensory hijack. Body-first, logic second.

One image. One sentence. Body-first.

Appendix: Anita Tang’s Rooftop Solar

Anita Tang (56) and her architect husband Stephen (62) installed solar panels at Fairview Park, Yuen Long, in October 2018.

Installation cost: HK$180,000

title: Our Response to Climate Change: Hong Kong 2100 - Renewable Energy & Transportation
text:
Appendix: Detailed Renewable Energy Data

Solar: Abundant sunlight; low operating costs. But: high initial costs, requires significant space, production variability. Duck Curve problem at scale.
Wind: Clean and renewable; offshore potential. But: high installation/maintenance costs, marine and bird life impact. Blade disposal problem.
Hydropower: Reliable, low GHG. But: ecosystem disruption, zero feasibility in HK.
Geothermal: Consistent, small footprint. But: wrong geology for HK. EMSD project is ground-source heat pumps only.
Biomass: Uses organic waste. But: deforestation risk, combustion emissions.
Tidal/Wave: Predictable, minimal visual impact. But: high cost, early-stage. CityU research exploring potential.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: Something You Took Away Last Week
text:
Something You Took Away Last Week

Week 1 gave you a formula: Fact + Human Story + Stakes = Spectacle.
You learned that facts alone don’t change minds. Stories do.

A graph of AI energy use? Forgettable.
A grandmother who can’t afford electricity because of a data centre next door? Unforgettable.



That’s your first portable tool. It works everywhere — and you’ll see it again today.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
From Debate → The Dinner Table

Last week: “Can humans out-persuade AI?”
This week: “What should you eat — and who decides?”
Same Spectacle Formula. New battlefield.
The food system is one of the most emotionally charged climate topics because it’s personal. Everyone eats. Everyone has opinions. And the data is… complicated.


Today’s conceptual tool: complexity. You’ll learn that there’s rarely one right answer — and that’s not a bug, it’s the point.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Before We Begin…

In 2013, a scientist grew a hamburger in a lab.
It cost $330,000.
A food critic tasted it on live TV. He paused. Then he said something that changed the meat industry forever.
I’ll tell you what he said at the end of today’s class.


(That’s an open loop. You’ll learn why it works today.)

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Two Sides. Two Food Futures.



PRO-CLIMATE


= Transform the Food System
= “Meat is murder (of the planet)”




PRO-DEVELOPMENT


= Improve, Don’t Replace
= “Let people eat what they want”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Core Tension








PRO-CLIMATE
PRO-DEVELOPMENT




Reduce meat consumption
Improve meat production


Plant-based is the future
Choice and tradition matter


Systemic dietary change
Individual freedom


Global emissions focus
Local livelihoods focus


Health + planet aligned
Economic stability priority



This tension defines every food policy debate.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: An Inconvenient Truth on Hong Kong Food
text:
An Inconvenient Truth on Hong Kong Food

Hong Kong imports 90% of its food.
If shipping stopped tomorrow, we’d have 2 weeks of reserves.
Every meal you eat traveled thousands of kilometers to reach your plate.


Now let’s talk about food systems.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:

Raise your hand if…

You ate meat in the last 24 hours
You could go a full month without meat
You’ve tried plant-based meat (Impossible, Beyond, OmniPork)
You’d eat lab-grown “real” meat if it was cheaper

Look around. Remember these numbers.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100

Three Contenders. One Plate.



🥩 Traditional Meat

“Real food for real people”




🥬 Plant-Based

“Save the planet, one bite at a time”




🧫 Lab-Grown

“Real meat, no slaughter”




Which one wins? It’s not as obvious as you think.

“Eat your vegetables” isn’t always climate advice.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
“Lab-Grown Meat Will Save Us”

The uncomfortable truth:

Lab-grown meat requires bioreactors running 24/7
Energy source matters: coal-powered lab meat could be worse than beef
Current estimates: 7-25 kg CO₂ per kg (vs. 60 for beef, 2 for tofu)
Scaling up is massively expensive — $300,000 for the first burger (2013)


The catch: Lab meat is only “green” if powered by renewable energy. Otherwise, you’re just trading cow farts for coal emissions.

“Plant-Based Meat Is Healthy & Green”

Not so fast:

Impossible Burger has more sodium than a Big Mac

Soy monocultures are destroying the Amazon too
Almond milk uses 6x more water than oat milk


The irony: The same people who won’t eat “processed food” are promoting Impossible Burgers as health food.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Dinner Table Dilemma

Scenario 1: Lab-grown char siu is now cheaper than real pork. It tastes identical. Your grandmother says she’d rather die than eat “fake meat” at Chinese New Year.
Do you serve it anyway?
Scenario 2: A study shows that if Hong Kong went fully vegetarian, we’d cut food emissions by 70%. But Sham Shui Po’s last dai pai dong — run by the same family for 60 years — would close.
Do you support the policy?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Ethics Get Messy



PRO-CLIMATE says:


“Tradition isn’t an excuse to destroy the planet. Your grandmother’s nostalgia doesn’t outweigh your grandchildren’s future.”




PRO-DEVELOPMENT says:


“You want to save the climate by erasing culture? My grandmother survived the Cultural Revolution. Now you want to take her char siu too?”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text: Two Magazines. Two Realities.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
What Do You Notice?

Look at the LEFT image (Pro-Climate):

What colors dominate? Why?
What’s the visual metaphor?
Who is the implied audience?
What emotion is it trying to trigger?

Look at the RIGHT image (Pro-Development):

How is food presented differently?
What values does the imagery appeal to?
What’s being defended?


Key question: Which cover would make YOUR grandmother pick up the magazine?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Today’s Debate Motion

“Hong Kong should ban the import of factory-farmed meat by 2035.”


PRO-CLIMATE must argue for the ban.
PRO-DEVELOPMENT must argue against.
You have the facts. Now make them feel something.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
PRO-CLIMATE: Make It Personal

Don’t say: “Livestock contributes to deforestation.”
Say: “Every burger in Hong Kong has a piece of the Amazon in it. You’re eating rainforest.”
Don’t say: “We should reduce meat consumption.”
Say: “Your grandparents ate meat once a week. Now you eat it twice a day. Guess which generation is destroying the planet?”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
PRO-DEVELOPMENT: Paint the Picture

Don’t say: “Dietary choices are personal.”
Say: “My grandmother survived famine. Now they want to tell her she can’t have pork at Chinese New Year? That’s not climate action — that’s cultural erasure.”
Don’t say: “Traditional farming supports livelihoods.”
Say: “Kill the cattle industry and you kill 50,000 farmers in the New Territories. Who retrains them? You? The activists?”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: Human Story: The Sai Kung Fisherman
text:
Human Story: The Sai Kung Fisherman


Uncle Wong, 63, has fished Hong Kong waters for 40 years. His father did the same. So did his grandfather. His boat is worth less than a parking space in Tai Koo.
Climate activists say his catch is unsustainable. Supermarkets want him to sell farmed salmon instead.
PRO-CLIMATE says: “The fish stocks are collapsing. Wong is part of the problem. Every wild fish he catches accelerates extinction.”
PRO-DEVELOPMENT says: “Wong’s family survived the Japanese occupation, the ’67 riots, and SARS. Now some NGO wants to end his livelihood because of a chart? He can barely afford rent.”

The real question: Who decides what “sustainable” means — the scientist, the politician, or Uncle Wong?

text:
Remember the $330,000 Burger?

The food critic took a bite. Paused. Then said:
“It’s close to meat. It’s not that juicy. But the bite feels like a conventional hamburger.”
Not a triumph. Not a disaster. Just… close.
That was 2013. Today, lab-grown meat costs under $10/kg to produce.
Singapore already sells it in restaurants. The US approved it in 2023.

The question isn’t whether it will replace your char siu.
The question is whether your grandmother will eat it.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Rhetorical Engineering 101

Throughout this course, you’ll learn 10 persuasion techniques — one per week.
Each technique is grounded in psychology and behavioral science. Each one is used by marketers, politicians, lawyers, and anyone who needs to move people.
By Week 11, you’ll have a complete rhetorical toolkit to engineer arguments that don’t just inform — they persuade.
The goal: Make your climate arguments impossible to ignore.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
How This Works

Each week, at the end of class:

The Hook — a grabby example that shows the technique in action
The Science — the psychology behind why it works
You Just Saw It — how it appeared in today’s debates
Next Week’s Challenge — try it yourself

At the start of the following week: Quick callback — did anyone try it?


Let’s begin.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Open Loop

In 1927, a psychologist noticed waiters could remember complex orders perfectly — until the bill was paid.
Then they forgot everything instantly.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Science

This is the Zeigarnik Effect.
The brain can’t let go of incomplete tasks. It allocates background processing — like a browser tab you can’t close.
Netflix forces a 3-second hook before “Skip Intro” appears. Marketers call this an open loop: start a pattern, don’t close it.
The brain will stay to close the loop.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
You Just Saw It

The best presentations today didn’t start with conclusions.
They started with a question. A tension. Something unresolved.
“There’s one fact that made me switch sides on this issue. I’ll get to it.”
Did you stay to hear it? That’s the loop.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Next Week’s Challenge

Open a loop in your first 10 seconds.
Don’t close it until the end.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Quick Callback

Last week’s strategy: The Cliffhanger Return
Your last line should echo your first — transformed.
Anyone try it? Did you bookend your presentation by returning to your opening image or story?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: Something You Took Away Last Week
text:
Something You Took Away Last Week

Week 10 gave you the full picture: cascade effects and tipping points.
Wolves reintroduced to Yellowstone literally changed the course of rivers. The Aral Sea disappeared because of cotton. Permafrost holds 1,700 gigatonnes of carbon — twice what’s in the atmosphere right now.
Everything connects. And some thresholds, once crossed, don’t come back.


So what if you could see all of those connections at once? What if you could see Earth from outside?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
section: From Cascade Effects → The View From Above
text:
From Cascade Effects → The View From Above

Last week: “Everything is connected — pull one thread, everything moves.”
This week: “What if perspective itself is the tool?”
The Overview Effect.
Astronauts who see Earth from space report a permanent shift in how they think about borders, nations, and environmental destruction. They call it the Overview Effect.
This is our final week. It’s not an accident that we end here.


Your full toolkit: Spectacle Formula → Complexity → System Boundaries → Timing → Built Environment → Structural Incentives → Epistemic Humility → Invisible Infrastructure → Responsibility Asymmetry → Cascade Effects → Perspective.

text:
2: Historical Context - The Original Space Race

US vs. USSR competition:

Sputnik launch (1957)
Moon landing (1969)

Symbolized:

National pride
Technological superiority



The space race was a 20th Century struggle between two nation-states, the Soviet Union (USSR) and the United States (US). The pursuit for both was the domination of space flight technologies. The competition began on 2 August 1955, when the Soviet Union responded to the US announcement of their similar intent to launch artificial satellites.
The space race between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) was a remarkable time in history with many far-reaching achievements in science, space exploration, and technology. This timeline shows the twenty-year competition between the two nations.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
3: Historical vs. Modern Space Race

Then: Government-led

Apollo program budget: ~$25.4 billion (~$150 billion in today’s dollars)
Eugene Cernan was the last person to walk on the moon moon in 1972.
Communication, medical and technological advancements
‘thaw’ in cold war led to cooperation between two nations



On 12 September 1962, President John F. Kennedy delivered a speech to a large crowd gathered at the Rice Stadium in Houston, Texas. The speech called for an ambitious space exploration programme that included not just missions to put astronauts on the Moon, but various other space projects such as communications and weather satellites. The quotation “We choose to go to the Moon” is remembered as the tagline from the speech. The US would succeed in this mission eight years later, but Kennedy would not be around to see it. He was assassinated two years later in Dallas, Texas on 22 November 1963.
How it was started: Following the Second World War, the United States and the Soviet Union competed to see who had the best technology in space. This included events such as the first satellite to orbit Earth, the first human-crewed spacecraft and the first person to walk on the Moon. The space race was considered vital by the two nations as it showed the world which country had the superior scientific, economic and political systems.


Now: Private sector involvement

SpaceX valuation: Over $100 billion as of 2021
Competitors catching up but not fast enough

text:
PRO-CLIMATE: Make It Personal

Don’t say: “Space exploration wastes resources.”
Say: “Jeff Bezos spent $5.5 billion on 11 minutes in space. That’s more than the entire annual budget for UNICEF clean water programs. You’re watching billionaires play astronaut while children die of thirst.”
Don’t say: “We should focus on Earth.”
Say: “Mars has no oxygen, no water, no life. Earth has all three — and we’re destroying them. The ‘backup planet’ fantasy is just rich people planning their escape.”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
PRO-DEVELOPMENT: Paint the Picture

Don’t say: “Space technology has spinoffs.”
Say: “GPS, weather satellites, fire detection from orbit — space technology saves more lives per year than all climate NGOs combined. You want to cut that?”
Don’t say: “Humans need a backup planet.”
Say: “The dinosaurs didn’t have a space program. They’re extinct. One asteroid and everything we’ve built — every symphony, every cure, every child’s laugh — gone forever. Space isn’t escape. It’s insurance.”

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:

Planetary Collective (~19 min). Five astronauts describe what happens when you see Earth from space. Watch the first 5-7 minutes — that’s enough to feel it.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
One Image. One Astronaut.

“You develop an instant global consciousness, a people orientation, an intense dissatisfaction with the state of the world, and a compulsion to do something about it.”
— Edgar Mitchell, Apollo 14


That’s not poetry. That’s a data point from someone who saw the system from outside.

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
Human Story: The Overview Effect

Every astronaut who sees Earth from space reports the same thing: profound awe at Earth’s fragility and unity. Borders disappear. The atmosphere looks impossibly thin.
PRO-CLIMATE says: “That’s the point! They realize Earth is all we have. Stop going to space — start protecting home.”
PRO-DEVELOPMENT says: “That’s also the point! Only by going to space do we truly understand Earth. The environmental movement was born from the Apollo 8 ‘Earthrise’ photo.”
The real question: Does space exploration wake us up to Earth’s fragility — or distract us from saving it?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Self-Persuasion Trick

People paid $1 to lie about a boring task later believed the task was interesting.
People paid $20 didn’t change their beliefs.
Why?

title: CCGL9065: Our Response to Climate Change: HK2100
text:
The Science

This is Cognitive Dissonance (Festinger, 1957).
$1 wasn’t enough justification, so the brain invented one: “I must have actually liked it.”
Cults don’t brainwash. They get you to do things.
Then your brain rewrites the story.
Behavior first. Belief follows.

They were when someone asked you to do something:
“Raise your hand if you’ve ever felt hopeless about climate.”
Now you’ve publicly identified. Your brain needs to reconcile that.

Make them say it. Make them do something.
They’ll persuade themselves.

What This Course Is Really About

We’re not here to recite science.

You’ll learn to construct, deconstruct, and weaponize stories for climate action.
